  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
       CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL  NO………….. OF 2026
(@Special Leave Petition (C)  No.21642/2026)
 
KHAJURAHO GREENS LLP & ORS.                     Appellant (s)
                                VERSUS
BESTCO CABLES PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR.           Respondent(s)
   O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal arises from the order passed by the High
Court of Judicature at Allahabad dated 29.5.2026 preferred by
the  respondents  before  us  under  Article  227  of  the
Constitution seeking appropriate relief in respect of Section
9 application preferred under the Arbitration and Conciliation
Act, 1996 (for short, “the Act, 1996”) before the Court in
NOIDA.  The High Court passed the following impugned order:-
“1.Heard Sri Anurag Khanna, learned senior counsel
assisted by Raghav Dev Garg, learned counsel for
the  petitioner  and  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent.
Learned senior counsel submits that an application
under  section  9  of  the  Arbitration  and
Reconciliation Act, 1996 has been filed which is
still  pending  before  the  Commercial  Court.  He
further  submits  that  the  respondent  has  filed
Matter Under Article 227 No 2792 of 2026 in which
interim protection has been granted by order dated
27.2.2026 staying the effect and operation of the
order dated 182.2026. He further submits that under
the grab of the said order the respondents are
proceeding to dispose of the property in question
as well as the Commercial Court is not deciding the
application filed under section 9 of the Act.
In view of the facts and circumstances of the case,
list this matter as fresh on 7.7.2026 along with
1

--- Page 223 ---
with connected matter.
Till the next date of listing the respondent shall
not create any third party right over the property
in question.”
3. The appellants being dissatisfied with the impugned order
passed by the High Court referred to above, are here before
us.
4. We heard Mr. Rajiv Shakdher, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellants and Mr. Ravindra Shrivastava, the
learned senior counsel appearing for the respondents.
5. It appears from the materials on record that there is one
petition preferred by the appellants before us, i.e., under
Article 227 of the Constitution being Petition No. 2792 of
2026 before the High Court.  This was first in point of time.
In the said petition, the appellants raised an issue with
regard to the territorial jurisdiction of the NOIDA Court to
adjudicate  the  Section  9  application  preferred  by  the
respondents. In the said petition, the very same learned Judge
of the High Court passed the following order on 27.02.2026:-
“1. Heard Sri Anil Bhushan, learned Senior Advocate
assisted by Sri Adarsh Bhushan, learned counsel for
the petitioner and Sri Vinay Khare, learned Senior
Advocate assisted by Sri Shikhar Kaushal, learned
counsel for respondents.
2. Sri Khare raises a preliminary objection about
the maintainability of the present writ petition
and submits that the present writ petition is not
maintainable in view of Section 8 read with Section
13 of the Commercial Courts Act and the petitioners
are liable to file appeal.
3. Confronted with the said fact, Sri Anil Bhushan,
learned Senior Advocate refers proviso to Section
13 and submits that the present writ petition is
maintainable.
2

--- Page 224 ---
4. In view of above, perusal of the record shows
that the present writ petition is maintainable as
the application of the petitioner filed under Order
VII Rule 10 as well as Section 151 C.P.C. for
return of application for want of jurisdiction, has
been rejected.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
a partnership agreement was executed on 27.08.2024
at Bhopal, M.P., which contemplates an arbitration
clause in para no. 13.4 and therefore, if any
dispute  arises  between  the  parties,  the
jurisdiction of M.P. Courts are there. He further
submits  that  the  respondent  nos.  1  &  2  were
expelled from the partnership firm on 27.01.2026.
The said order has not been assailed but an appeal
has been filed under Section 9 with the relief
which cannot be granted by the Commercial Court.
The matter ought to have been referred to the
arbitrator as contemplated in the agreement. In
support of his submission, he has relied upon the
judgment passed by Hon'ble Supreme Court in the
case of Ravi Ranjan Development Pvt. Ltd. versus
Aditya  Kumar  Chatterjee,  reported  in  2022  SCC
OnLine SC 568.
6. The said fact has seriously been opposed by the
learned counsel for respondents and submits that
Section 16 of the C.P.C. empowers the filing of
present  application  and  submits  that  part  of
jurisdiction is within the territorial jurisdiction
of the Noida Court as the property belongs to
partnership firm is situated in Noida, therefore,
has rightly filed the appeal before the Commercial
Court.
7. The matter requires consideration.
8.  The  respondents  may  file  counter  affidavit
within a period of four weeks. Rejoinder affidavit,
if any, may be filed within a week, thereafter.
9. List thereafter.
10. Till the next date of listing, the impugned
order shall be kept in abeyance.”
6. Thus, on one hand, the appellants, first in point of
time,  raised  an  issue  with  regard  to  the  territorial
jurisdiction of the NOIDA Court to adjudicate the application
under Section 9  and later in point of time, the respondents
3

--- Page 225 ---
before us moved a petition for expeditious hearing of the
Section 9 petition before the NOIDA Court.
7. Prima facie, we are of the view that the very procedure
adopted by the High Court is not correct.
8. In fact, there was no good reason for the respondents to
prefer any petition before the High Court under Article 227 of
the Constitution for expeditious disposal of their Section 9
petition.
9. They should have appeared in the very same first petition
preferred  by  the  appellants  and  should  have  raised  all
contentions available to them in law.
10. We are of the view that let both the petitions referred
to above be heard on 07.08.2026 without fail.
11. Urgent hearing of these two petitions is necessary to
avoid further complications in the matter.
12.  The impugned order passed by the High Court is hereby
set aside.
13. A strong apprehension has been expressed by the learned
senior counsel appearing for the respondents that if the
interim order  is vacated , the appellants may create third
party rights.
14. It is needless to clarify that pending the proceedings
before the Court if there is any alienation or any third party
rights are created, the same would always be subject to lis
pendens.
15. Having  appeared  in  the  petition  preferred  by  the
appellants first in point of time, we are of the view that
4

--- Page 226 ---
there was no good reason or ground for the respondents to
prefer their 227 petition.
16. With the aforesaid, the appeal stands disposed of.
17. Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.
...................J.
[J.B.PARDIWALA]
...................J.
[K. VINOD CHANDRAN]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15,2026.

KHAJURAHO GREENS LLP & ORS.                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
BESTCO CABLES PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR.             Respondent(s)

--- Page 229 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No……….. of 2026
[ @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 ]
V.P . CHITRA
…APPELLANT
Versus
THE STATE, REP . BY THE INSPECTOR
OF POLICE & ANR.                                             
…RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is the wife of the second respondent
aggrieved with the prayer for de-freezing a bank account
and  release of properties seized having been denied by
the  learned  Judicial  Magistrate  -  1,  Kanchipuram  in  a
petition  under  Section  451  of  the  Cr.P .C.  which  was
affirmed in the impugned judgment by the learned Single
Judge of the High Court.
3. The appellant is maintaining a savings bank account
with  the  Karur  Vysya  Bank,  Redhills  Branch,  bearing
Account No.1664155000060896  which  has been  frozen
and 18 sovereigns of gold jewellery of the appellant as
also original title deeds of the appellant’s father-in-law
and  the  sister-in-law’s  husband  and  certain  other
Page 1 of 7
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 

--- Page 230 ---
documents as also death certificate, legal heir certificate
and other office files and personal files kept in the bank
locker were seized on orders of the Investigating Officer
(I.O).  The details of the articles seized as available in the
locker maintained by them in the Bank, are available in
the letter addressed by the Branch Manager of the Karur
Vysya  Bank  to  the  Inspector  of  Police,  Central  Crime
Branch as extracted in the impugned order.
4. The allegation as discernible from the records is that
the second respondent was appointed and continued as a
temporary accountant in a temple. When verification of
accounts  were  done  forgery  and  misappropriation  was
deducted. The second respondent is said to have then
attacked the Executive Officer based on which a case was
registered before the jurisdictional Police Station. Later
on, regarding the misappropriation, Crime No.184/2019
was registered and a charge-sheet has been filed.  It is
alleged  that  more  than  Rs.50,00,000/-  was
misappropriated from the temple, which allegations led to
the seizure of the articles in the locker and the freezing of
the account.
Page 2 of 7
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 

--- Page 231 ---
5. We notice from the impugned order that the account
contained  only  Rs.2,000/-  making  the  freezing
inconsequential, if not futile. Insofar as the jewellery it is
the claim of the appellant that it belongs to her, having
been gifted by her parents at the time of her marriage.
Insofar as the documents are concerned, we cannot but
notice that some are prior to the commencement of the
service of the second respondent in the temple, which is
said to have commenced 10 years prior to 2019.  The
other  seized documents are also  in the name of third
parties though close relatives.  
6. If at all proceedings are taken against the assets of
the second respondent, it has to be proved to have been
acquired  from  the  misappropriated  amounts,  which
misappropriation  has  to  be  proved  first.  Further  only
through  a  civil  suit,  where  the  misappropriation  is
established and the acquisition of property based on the
proceeds of such misappropriation proved, could there be
a recovery effected.  The mere freezing of an account
having  Rs.2,000/-  balance  or  keeping  attached  the
ornaments  and  the  documents  available  in  the  Bank
locker in a criminal case would not serve any purpose.
Page 3 of 7
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 

--- Page 232 ---
The documents of title prior to the petitioner’s service
can be of no use in the criminal prosecution and those in
the  name  of  close  relatives  during  the  period  of  his
service,  could  be  at  best  be  used  to  allege  benami
transactions on the proceeds of misappropriation.  The
seizure  of  documents  is  unnecessary  and  in  that
circumstance, on release, duly attested true copy of the
documents can be retained by the Magistrate’s Court.
7. In the above circumstances, we are of the opinion
that there is no reason to continue the freezing of the
account or seizure of the articles.  The orders passed by
the learned Judicial Magistrate Court and the High Court
are set aside.  We direct the de-freezing of the account,
the number of which is mentioned above and set aside
the seizure of the articles in the locker.  The Bank shall
immediately  de-freeze  the  account  and  release  the
articles  including  jewellery  and  the  documents  in  the
locker to the appellant or if the same has been produced
before  the  Magistrate  it  shall  be  released  by  the
Magistrate’s  Court  on  appropriate  acknowledgement
received from the appellant.  If the release is made by the
bank,  it  shall  be  ensured  that  the  copies  of  the
Page 4 of 7
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 

--- Page 233 ---
documents are attested by the Branch Manager and the
persons in whose name the locker is maintained, which
shall  be  produced  by  the  Branch  Manager  before  the
Magistrate’s  Court.   If  the  documents  were  produced
before the Magistrate’s Court, true copies with attestation
of the accused shall be obtained for retention before the
Court and the original shall be released.
8. We make it clear that we have not spoken on the
merits  of  the  allegations  levelled  against  the  second
respondent; neither on the misappropriation nor on the
illegal  acquisition,  which  would  be  adjudicated
independently.
9. The appeal stands allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
.……….…….……….……. J.
                       (J.B. PARDIWALA)
.……….…….……….……. J.
        (K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI
JULY 17, 2026.

Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024

 (Signed order is placed on the file)
Page 7 of 7
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11879 of 2024 

--- Page 236 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.        /2026  
@ DIARY NO.16370/2026
YOGIDAS BAIRAGI ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Delay in filing and refiling is condoned.
3. Leave granted.
4. Appellant who has been tried for an offense
punishable under Sections 454, 451, and 354 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short “IPC”)
read  with  section  3(1)(xi)  of  the  Scheduled
Castes  and  Scheduled  Tribes  (Prevention  of
Atrocities) Act, 1989 (for short “SC/ST Act”) and
was convicted and sentenced to undergo one year
rigorous  imprisonment  for  offense  punishable
under Section 454 of the IPC, six months rigorous
1

--- Page 237 ---
imprisonment for offense punishable under Section
451  of  the  IPC,  and  six  months  rigorous
imprisonment for offense punishable under Section
354  of  the  IPC,  and  six  months  rigorous
imprisonment  with  a  fine  of  Rs.200/-  under
section  3(1)(xi)  of  the  SC/ST  Act.  All  the
sentences were ordered to run concurrently.
5. Being aggrieved by the same, an appeal came
to be filed before the High Court, which after
examination  and  reappreciation  of  evidence,
acquitted the accused for the offense punishable
under the SC/ST Act, and upheld the convictions
under the IPC, and reduced the jail sentence to
one day already undergone and directed payment of
Rs5,000/- as compensation payable to complainant
within two months, with a default sentence to
undergo the remaining jail sentence awarded by
the trial court in the event of non-payment of
fine. Hence, this appeal.
6. Ms. Ananya Sikri, learned counsel appearing
for the appellant has vehemently contended that
2

--- Page 238 ---
there  are  material  inconsistencies  in  the
testimonies of Pappi Bai, Koyali Bai and Guddi
Bai, whose statements have been relied upon by
the Courts to convict the accused. The said
witnesses  all  being  closely  related  to  the
complainant,  and  there  being  no  independent
corroboration despite the alleged incident having
taken place at a thickly populated area, their
evidence cannot be accepted. Hence, she seeks for
allowing the appeal.
7. Per contra , the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent – state has supported the
judgment of the court below and submits that the
appellant, for having not paid the compensation
amount as ordered by the appellate court which
was after taking note of the financial condition
of the appellant had awarded a sum of Rs.5,000/-
as compensation, which was also not paid, and as
such, the sentences imposed by the trial court
would automatically come into force and hence,
the appeal being without merits, be dismissed.
3

--- Page 239 ---
8. Having heard the learned counsels appearing
for the parties and on perusal of the records, we
notice that undisputedly, the statement of the
witnesses recorded before the trial court, which
was relied upon by both the courts to convict the
accused, were closely related. That by itself
would not be a ground to discard their evidence
or disbelieve their statements. Even otherwise,
the appellate court took a lenient view and
reduced the punishment to one day imprisonment,
which  the  appellant  had  already  undergone.
However, in lieu of the same, it has ordered
payment of fine of Rs.5,000/- as compensation
payable to the complainant. Yet, the said amount
has not been paid by the appellant. The very fact
that the appellant has not been able to mobilize
a  paltry  sum  of  Rs.5,000/-,  itself  would
reinforce the argument of the appellant that he
is in penury or in financial distress. It is for
this reason he has been taken into custody on
14/10/2025 and is in jail till date, that is
4

--- Page 240 ---
almost nine months. In that view of the matter,
we are of the considered view that the judgment
of the High Court deserves to be affirmed and
sentence modified, namely, the sentence awarded
is modified to the effect that the period of
sentence already undergone by the appellant from
14/10/2025 till date would suffice in view of
non-payment of compensation of Rs.5,000/- payable
to the complainant. Accordingly, the appeal is
allowed. The impugned order is modified, and
appellant  is  ordered  to  be  set  at  liberty
forthwith, if not, required in any other case.
9. Pending  applications  stand  consigned  to
records. No order as to costs.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.
5

--- Page 241 ---

1. Leave granted.
2. The High Court of Judicature at Allahabad,
by  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  dated
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.10118/2026 1

--- Page 244 ---
18th December,  2025,  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for regular bail.
3. Appellant,  figuring as  an accused  in FIR
No.0148  of  2023  dated  12th May,  2023
registered at Police Station Manduadih, District
Varuna, Uttar Pradesh under Sections 3(2)(va),
3(1)(gha) & 3(1)(da) of the Scheduled Castes
and Scheduled T ribes (Prevention of Atrocities)
Act,  1989  (Amendment  2015),  Sections  506,
504, 323 & 147 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860,
was arrested on the same date.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
5. We find from the counter affidavit of the
respondent-State  that  the  allegation  against
the appellant is of assault on the victim with a
brick to cause death.
6. It  appears  that  the  appellant  has  been
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.10118/2026 2

--- Page 245 ---
incarcerated for over three years, although the
trial  is  yet  to  commence.  For  framing  of
charges, tomorrow is the date fixed. 
7. Having  regard  to  the  long  period  of
incarceration  of  the  appellant  without  the
possibility of early completion of the trial, we
find  that  his  further  detention  in  custody  is
unnecessary; hence, we are inclined to accept
the  appeal  and  grant  relief  as  prayed  by
admitting the appellant to an order for grant of
bail.
8. Accordingly,  we  set  aside  the  impugned
judgment and order.
9. Appellant shall be released on bail, subject
to furnishing of bail bonds to the satisfaction of
the trial court and subject to such other terms
and conditions as may be imposed by it.
10. Needless  to observe,  the  appellant shall
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.10118/2026 3

--- Page 246 ---
not,  directly  or  indirectly,  by  making
inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case
from disclosing such facts to the court.
11. In the  event there  is  any breach of the
terms and conditions for grant of bail, the trial
court shall be at liberty to cancel the bail of the
appellant.
12. It is also ordered that the appellant shall
diligently  attend  proceedings  of  the  trial,
unless exempted. If he is found to abstain from
attending  the  proceedings  without  justifiable
cause, that could also be seen as breach of the
conditions for grant of bail and the trial court
will be free to pass appropriate orders.
13. We clarify that the observations made in
this order and grant of bail will not be treated
as findings on the merits of the case.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.10118/2026 4

--- Page 247 ---
14. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the
aforesaid terms.
15. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

    Mr. Devraj Bhattacharjee, Adv.
Ms. Radhika Gautam, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Parmanand Gaur, AOR
                   Ms. Megha Gaur, Adv.
                   Mr. Vibhav Mishra, Adv.
                                      
                   Mr. Rohan Dewan, AOR
                   Mr. Devvrat, Adv.
Ms. Harshita Sharma, Adv.
Mr. Rudra Deosthali, aDv.
                   Mr. Siddhartha Iyer, AOR
                   Mr. Aman Gupta, Adv.
                   Ms. Jaispriya Poply, Adv.
                   Ms. Srishti Ghoshal, Adv.
                   
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The High Court of Delhi while dealing with a suo moto petition
registered pursuant to suicide committed by a law student rendered
2

--- Page 252 ---
a judgment dated 3 rd November, 2025, wherein at para 249, certain
directions were issued in respect of attendance norms in the law
colleges.  The  sum  and  substance  of  the  aforesaid  directions
mandates the Bar Council of India to undertake a re-evaluation of
the mandatory attendance norms for the 3-year and 5-year L.L.B.
courses in India in line with the observations made by the High
Court in the preceding paras of the judgment and also in line with
the NEP-2020 and so also the 2003 UGC regulations which contemplate
flexibility in attendance requirements. Further, the High Court
forbade  any  law  college/university/institution  in  India  from
detaining  any  student  enrolled  in  any  recognised  law
college/university/institution from taking examinations or from
being  prevented  to  take  further  academic  pursuits  or  career
progression on the ground of lack of minimum attendance. 
2. Consequent to the aforesaid judgment, the Bar Council of India
issued  a  letter  to  various  colleges/universities/institutions
imparting law courses, directing that no student should be detained
from appearing in examinations or prevented from progressing to the
next semester solely on the ground of shortage of attendance during
the ongoing period in which the Bar Council of India would re-
evaluate the attendance norms pursuant to the Delhi High Court’s
judgment.
3. As a consequence, it is reported that many students pursuing
law courses stopped attending classes on a regular basis for
various reasons.  This Court took up the issue of dilution of the
3

--- Page 253 ---
attendance norms in SLP (Crl.) No. 9450 of 2026 preferred by the
petitioner - SVKMS Narsee Monjee Institute of Management Studies,
and passed an interim order dated 26 th May, 2026 in the following
terms: -
“List on 21.07.2026. 
In the meantime, the effect and operation of the
paragraph no. 249 of the impugned judgment shall
remain  stayed,  however  the  same  shall  be
effective prospectively.
In the meantime, it would be open for the High
Courts where similar matters are pending on the
issue of attendance, to take an appropriate
final decision. The pendency of this petition
may not come in the way of the High Courts to
decide the matters on merits.”
4. Pursuant to the above order, a situation has arisen wherein
some colleges/universities propose to detain/prevent the students
having  shortfall  in  attendance  from  appearing  in  the  final
examinations/supplementary examinations for the ongoing academic
session.  Numerous applications have been filed before this Court
and writ petitions have been filed by such students before High
Courts praying for relaxation of the mandatory attendance norms on
the ground that, during the contemporary academic session, there
prevailed a state of uncertainty on account of the Delhi High
Court’s judgment and hence, the students started entertaining a
legitimate expectation that they would not be barred from appearing
in the examination on account of shortage of attendance. It has,
thus, been prayed that, for the preceding academic year, the
colleges imparting courses in law be restrained from detaining the
4

--- Page 254 ---
students on account of shortage of attendance.
5. Shri Mukul Rohatgi, learned senior counsel representing the
petitioner in SLP (Crl.) 9450 of 2026 has vehemently opposed the
submissions advanced by learned counsel for the applicants.
6. He urged that 6 different High Courts have taken a view
contrary to the one taken by the Delhi High Court  (supra) on
attendance norms and hence, the students cannot be allowed to take
advantage of the Delhi High Court judgment and they have to face
the lawful consequences on account of shortage of attendance.
7. Having considered the submissions advanced at the bar, we are
of the considered view that as the judgment rendered by the Delhi
High Court in November, 2025 diluted the attendance norms in the
law colleges and as the directions contained in the said judgment
were virtually accepted by the Bar Council of India, the students
who acted under a bona fide belief that mere shortage of attendance
would not be an impediment to their appearing in the examinations
for the ongoing academic session are entitled to the benefit of
doubt as a one time measure only.  Otherwise also, we may clarify
that our stay order dated 26 th May, 2026 categorically records that
the effect thereof shall be prospective.
8.  Hence, we hereby direct and provide that the students whose
academic session was ongoing when the judgment dated 3 rd November,
2025 was rendered by the Delhi High Court, shall not be detained or
prevented from appearing in the final examinations for the said
academic session.  The order dated 26 th May, 2026 being prospective
5

--- Page 255 ---
in its operation, the law students who are now facing an imminent
consequence of loss of academic session on account of shortage of
attendance shall not be prejudiced by the order passed by this
Court and that they shall be permitted to appear in the final
examination for the said academic session.  In case the students
have not been able to appear in the final examination either
completely  or  partially,  they  shall  be  allowed  to  take  the
supplementary examination for this session only.
9. List again on 25th August, 2026 for final disposal.
(NEETU KHAJURIA)
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR-CUM-PS
      (RANJANA SHAILEY)
     ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
6

--- Page 256 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.  7055/2026
ALAMGIR SARDAR ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF WEST BENGAL ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. On a secret information received that two
persons, namely, Krishanu Biswas and Priya Biswas
were  carrying  Heroin  for  sell  at  Kalyani
Expressway, they were intercepted and arrested.
After 57 days of their arrest and recording their
statements on 25.03.2025, the appellant herein
came to be arrested on 21.05.2025.
4. It is the case of the prosecution that the
appellant was in touch with the persons who were
apprehended and was indulging in sale of drugs
1

--- Page 257 ---
which was admitted by him after being arrested.
Chargesheet having been filed, the jurisdictional
Court  has  taken  cognizance  and  none  of  the
witnesses have been examined as on date. The
application  for  grant  of  regular  bail  filed
before the trial court and the jurisdictional
High Court has been rejected and hence, this
appeal.
5. Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
has contended that there is no proximity or nexus
that would establish that appellant was indulging
in sale of Heroin seized from accused Nos.1 and
2. He would submit that based on co-accused
statement, appellant has been arrested. Hence, he
seeks for grant of regular bail.
6. Per contra, learned counsel appearing for
the respondent – State would vehemently contend
that the courts-below having taken note of the
totality of the circumstances of the case have
rightly refused the regular bail and hence, he
seeks for the rejection of the prayer for regular
2

--- Page 258 ---
bail.
7. Having heard the learned counsels appearing
for the parties and after perusing the entire
records, we notice that the contentions raised by
the learned counsel appearing for the appellant
requires to be accepted and there is no criminal
antecedent attributable to the appellant. It is
an undisputed fact that after 57 days of the
recording the statement of accused Nos.1 and 2
and their arrest, appellant has been roped in as
an accused on the premise that he was in touch
with the accused persons. As to what was the
actual  role  played  which  is  connecting  the
appellant to the seizure of the 349 gms of Heroin
from accused Nos.1 and 2 is an issue which will
have to be thrashed out after considering the
evidence that may be tendered by the prosecution
during the course of the trial. We do not propose
to enter into the scrutiny of the same as it is
likely to prejudice the rights of either of the
parties. In the facts and circumstances of the
3

--- Page 259 ---
case, we are of the considered view that the
appellant is entitled for release on regular bail
by the trial court on such terms and conditions
as  it  deems  fit  including  the  condition  of
directing the appellant to appear before the
trial court on all dates of hearing except when
physically  exempted.  Accordingly,  appeal  is
allowed. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 20, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NOS. 8608-8609 OF 2011
KRISHAN MURARI & ORS. ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
GOVT. OF NCT OF DELHI & ORS.  ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. The issue involved in these appeals relates to revision of pay
scale. Facts in brief leading to filing of these appeals are
crystallized as under:
  Union of India issued a recommendation on 12.05.1969 by which six
posts of Demonstrators were created and these posts were clarified
to be equivalent to Havaldar. Appellant Nos.1 to 4 were appointed
to the post of Demonstrators. Deputy Commandant General Home
Guards-cum-Deputy Director Civil Defence Delhi issued office order
dated 22.11.1988 for revision of pay scale of Demonstrators. On
30.07.1990,  the  Deputy  Secretary  Home  (General)  Delhi
Administration  Delhi  recommended  revision  of  pay  scale  of
1

--- Page 263 ---
Demonstrators in Delhi Home Guard & Civil Defense to the Director
(Delhi).  Several  rounds  of  letters  were  addressed  to  the
authorities. On 29.10.1998 (Annex. P-12), the Deputy Director Civil
Defence rejected the claim of the appellants herein for revision of
pay scale on the ground that there are no parallel in Recruitment
Rules of Demonstrators working in the Home Guards Department of
Govt of NCT Delhi and those working in NCDC, Nagpur.
3. Aggrieved by the same, the appellants approached the Central
Administrative  Tribunal  (CAT)  in  OA  No.114/2001  claiming  the
revision of pay of the Demonstrators of Home Guards Civil Defence
and  quashing  of  the  order  dated  29.10.1998  whereunder  the
representations of the appellants were rejected. The Tribunal by
its order dated 01.04.2002 allowed the applications by arriving at
a conclusion that neither of the parties had produced cogent
evidence to show or justify pay parity between Demonstrators of
Home Guards, to which category the appellants belong to being
equivalent to category of Demonstrators working in NCDC Nagpur.
However, a direction was issued to respondents herein to implement
the contents of their own letter dated 27.10.1990 wef date of issue
or wef date of applicants appointment whichever is later.
2

--- Page 264 ---
4. This order was challenged in W.P.(C) No.6395/2002 before the
High Court of Delhi, which came to be dismissed vide order dated
03.11.2003. On being challenged in Civil Appeal No. 287/2005, this
Court set aside the impugned order and remitted the matter back to
the High court for consideration afresh. By order dated 25.01.2011
writ petition came to be dismissed. A Review Petition filed
thereunder, also came to be dismissed on 10.02.2011. Hence, the
present appeals.
5. It is the contention of the learned counsel for the appellants
that letter dated 27.12.1990 issued by the Director General of
Civil Defence proposing to consider the revision of pay of the
Demonstrators was keeping in view the pay scale of Havaldar in HGCD
Delhi.  He  would  contend  that  the  post  of  Demonstrators  are
equivalent to the post of Havaldar, which post is available in HGCD
Delhi, and this fact has not been appreciated by the learned High
Court. He would also submit that Demonstrators working in other
States are having the pay scale 1200-2040 (pre-revised) and they
are similarly placed as that of the appellants, and as such, the
impugned order is liable to be set aside. Per contra the learned
counsel appearing for the respondents would support the impugned
order.
3

--- Page 265 ---
6. Having heard the learned counsels appearing for the parties and
after bestowing our careful and anxious consideration to rival
contentions raised at the Bar, we are not inclined to entertain the
plea forwarded by the appellants for reasons more than one,
firstly,  the  communication  dated  27.12.1990  relied  upon  very
heavily by the appellants as well as by the tribunal, is on a
misplaced notion. We say so, for the simple reason that it is
construed by these two forums as a communication forwarded for pay
revision. On a perusal of the said communication and as rightly
observed by the Division Bench of the High Court, it is not an
order but only a recommendation which ultimately did not get
crystallized by way of an order. Even otherwise, the said letter
which states that the pay of the Demonstrators may be revised,
keeping in view the scale of pay of the post of Havaldar, did not
find favour by the Finance Department, which had replied stating
that the Union of India has not delegated powers to create such
posts.  Secondly, the Fifth Pay Commission which was seized of this
matter, namely, the revision of pay scales of Demonstrators, also
did not recommend for the pay revision. To say the least, the Union
of India by letter dated 07.07.2004 forwarded to GNCTD, it was
informed that there is no justification for equating the two posts
even though they carry the same nomenclature. In other words, the
correspondence  that  ensued  for  pay  revision,  did  not  attain
4

--- Page 266 ---
finality. When the Fifth Pay Commission examined this issue in
detail, has rejected the prayer for revision of pay scale, the
contention of the learned counsel for the appellants cannot be
accepted.
7. For the reasons aforestated, we are not inclined to interfere
with the impugned order. Hence, the appeals stand dismissed. No
order as to costs.
8. Pending applications stand consigned to records.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
New Delhi;
July 16, 2026.
5

--- Page 267 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

CRIMINAL APPEAL No.3260 OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.7606/2026]
ASHOK SAHNI                 APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB    RESPONDENT
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The  High  Court  of  Punjab  &  Haryana  at
Chandigarh,  by  the  impugned  judgment  and  order
dated 18 th March, 2026 has rejected the appellant’s
prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest. 
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No.0237
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.7606/2026
1

--- Page 281 ---
of  2025  dated  10th December,  2025  registered  at
Police  Station  Macchiwara,  District  Khanna  under
Section 108 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS),
2023.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for
the  appellant  as  well  as  the  respondent-State  of
Punjab.
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant has joined
investigation in terms of the earlier order of this
Court. In such circumstances and having regard to
the  nature  of  accusations  levelled  and  more
particularly the role attributed to the appellant, we
see  no  reason  not  to  grant  relief  as  prayed  by
admitting him to an order for grant of bail.
6.  We, accordingly,  set aside  the  judgment and
order impugned in this appeal.
7. It is directed that in the event of the appellant
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.7606/2026
2

--- Page 282 ---
being arrested, he shall be released on bail on terms
and conditions to be imposed by the trial court.
8. Since  the investigation is yet to conclude, we
direct that if the investigating officer calls upon the
appellant  to  continue  to  join  the  investigation,  he
shall  do  so  by  attending  the  police  station  failing
which liberty is granted to bring any such omission to
the notice of the trial court and seek cancellation of
bail.
9. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,
directly or indirectly, by making inducement, threat
or promise, dissuade any person acquainted with the
facts of the case from disclosing such facts to any
police officer or to the court.
10. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in  this
order and grant of bail to the appellant in anticipation
of arrest will not be treated as findings on the merits
of the case.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.7606/2026
3

--- Page 283 ---
11. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed  on  the
aforesaid terms.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).       /2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 8031-8032/2026
RAHUL SINGH & ANR.                                 Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH                         Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard  learned  counsel  for  the
parties.
3. These appeal(s) arise from an order
dated 26.03.2026 passed by the High Court of
Judicature  at  Allahabad  rejecting  the
regular bail prayer(s) of the appellant(s)
in connection with Case Crime No. 319/2025,
registered at P.S. New Agra, District Agra.
4.  The  submission  on  behalf  of  the
appellant(s)  is  that  according  to  the
prosecution case, the victim was abducted on
22.09.2025; First Information Report (“FIR”)
1

--- Page 293 ---
was lodged on 24.09.2025; and on 25.09.2025,
the victim was recovered from a Hyundai i20
vehicle which was being driven by co-accused
Monu. Monu happens to be a constable in the
U.P. Police force. It is the case of the
appellant(s) that neither any weapon nor any
incriminating article was recovered from the
appellant(s)  and  their  case  is
distinguishable from Monu who was the driver
of the vehicle. It is also the case of the
appellant(s)  that  they  have  no  previous
criminal  antecedents  and  they  have  been
falsely  implicated.  Lastly,  it  has  been
contended  that  the  appellant(s)  have
suffered incarceration since 25.09.2025 and
if released on bail, they shall not misuse
the liberty. 
5. The  learned  Counsel  for  the  State-
respondent opposes the prayer for bail but
could not demonstrate that any incriminating
article was recovered from the possession of
the  appellant(s)  though  the  appellant(s)
were found present in the vehicle from which
2

--- Page 294 ---
victim was recovered. 
6.  Having  regard  to  the  facts  and
circumstances  of  the  case,  without
expressing  any  opinion  on  the  merits
thereof,  in  our  view,  the  case  of  the
appellant(s) might be distinguishable from
that of the main accused-Monu who was the
person in control of the vehicle from where
the victim was allegedly recovered.
7. In  such  circumstances  and  having
regard to the fact that the appellant(s)
have no previous criminal antecedents, we
are of the view that the appellant(s) are
entitled to be released on bail at this
stage.
8. Accordingly,  the  appeal(s)  are
allowed. The order passed by the High Court
rejecting the regular bail prayer(s) of the
appellant(s) is set aside.
9. The appellant(s) shall be released on
bail on such terms and conditions as the
trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
3

--- Page 295 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.
  
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………..J
[VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi;
July 20, 2026

Criminal Appeal  No(s).      /2026
@   SLP(Crl) No.   146/2026  
TANVI NIKHIL GUPTA                                Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR.                      Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted. 
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 19.12.2025 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay rejecting the
anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in connection with
C.R. No. 51/2025, registered at P.S. Lonikand, District Pune
city for the offences punishable under Sections 316(2),
316(3), 318(4) and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
(“BNS”).
4. After considering the submissions of learned counsel for
the appellant, on 07.01.2026, an interim order was passed,
which is reproduced below:
“1. Learned counsel for the petitioner
submitted  that  the  husband  of  the
petitioner was arrested and has already
been released on bail. The petitioner
herself was granted interim bail by the
1

--- Page 299 ---
High Court on 13.05.2025, subject to the
condition  that  she  shall  attend  the
police station every Monday of each week.
She duly complied with the direction and
cooperated with investigation until the
application for pre-arrest bail came to
be dismissed on 19.12.2025. 
2.  Though  the  FIR  was  registered  on
15.01.2025, the chargesheet has not been
filed till date.
3.  Issue  notice  to  the  respondents,
returnable on 13.02.2026.
4. Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, learned
Advocate-on-Record,  accepts  notice  on
behalf of the respondent NO.1.
5. Service on respondent No.2 be effected
through  the  concerned  Station  House
Officer.
6. In  the meantime,  in the  event of
arrest, the petitioner shall be released
on bail on furnishing bail bonds to the
satisfaction of the Arresting Officer in
connection with FIR No.0051 of 2025 dated
15th January, 2025 registered at Lonikand
Police  Station,  District  Pune  City,
Maharashtra. Needless to add that the
petitioner  shall  cooperate  with  the
investigation.”
5. Thereafter, vide order dated 13.02.2026, on the request
of the parties, the matter was referred to the Supreme Court
Mediation Centre. The said order is reproduced below:
2

--- Page 300 ---
“On joint request of the parties, we refer
the dispute to the Supreme Court Mediation
Center. 
The parties shall virtually appear before
the  Coordinator  of  the  Supreme  Court
Mediation Center on 26th February, 2026 at
2.30 p.m. 
In case personal presence of the parties is
required, the parties will abide by the
directions of the learned Mediator. 
The  learned  Mediator,  after  making  an
endeavour for amicable resolution of the
dispute, shall submit a report to this
Court. 
The Registry shall ensure that a copy of
this order along with relevant documents is
forwarded to the Coordinator, Supreme Court
Mediation Center at the earliest. 
List on 4th May, 2026.”
6. A mediated Settlement has been placed before us. Under
the said Settlement, parties have prayed for quashing of
certain other proceedings pending inter se parties. Besides,
the appellant has also agreed to pay Rs.38,00,000/- to the
second respondent (“the informant”). Out of which, a sum of
Rs.8,00,000/- has already been paid by a demand draft dated
08.05.2026 drawn on Yes Bank, a copy of which is enclosed
with the Settlement Agreement. The balance of Rs.30,00,000/-
is  being  paid  through  demand  draft  No.  535635,  dated
17.07.2026, drawn in favour of the second respondent (“Gaurav
3

--- Page 301 ---
Gera”). The learned counsel for the second respondent has
accepted the aforesaid draft.
7. In view of above, we deem it appropriate to dispose of
this appeal by making the interim order absolute, subject to
the following conditions:
a) Within  three  weeks  from  today,  the
appellant shall submit bail bonds to the
satisfaction of the Trial Court along
with an undertaking that she shall co-
operate in the trial/investigation; and 
b) She  shall  submit  an  additional
undertaking that she will not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. In respect of the prayer to quash other proceedings
pending inter se parties, we leave it open for the parties to
take recourse to appropriate remedy before the jurisdictional
High  Court.  The  parties  are  also  permitted  to  obtain
certified copy of the mediated Settlement for presentation in
the concerned court.
9.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO.181/2024
GARGI CHAUHAN                                     PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
AMITENDRA SINGH CHAUHAN & ANR.*                   RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
This Transfer Petition has been filed by the petitioner
who is the wife of the respondent seeking the following
reliefs:
(i) Transfer the HMA Case No.1717 of 2022 filed u/s 13(1)(ia)
Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 titled as ‘Amitendra Singh Chauhan
Vs. Gargi Chauhan & Ors.” from the Court of Addl. Principal
Judge Family Court, Gurugram, Haryana to the Principal
Judge, Family Court, District Gautam Budh Nagar, Uttar
Pradesh.
(ii) Pass such other or further order/orders as this Hon’ble
Court may deem fit in the larger interest of justice
considering the facts and circumstances of the present
case.
During the pendency of this Transfer Petition before this
Court, the matter was referred to the Supreme Court Mediation
Centre.
Learned counsel for the respective parties state that the
parties have arrived at a settlement and the terms of the
settlement are crystallized  in the Settlement Agreement dated
24.04.2025. 
1

--- Page 305 ---
The  parties  have  decided  to  part  ways  and  seek
dissolution of their marriage by a decree of divorce by mutual
consent on certain terms and conditions. That the respondent
has agreed to pay and has paid a sum of Rs.2,50,00,000/-(Rupees
Two crores and Fifty lakhs Only) to the petitioner-wife in full
and final settlement of all her claims and towards the claims
of the children. That the teenage daughters shall remain in the
permanent  custody  of  the  petitioner-wife  subject  to
visitation rights being granted to the respondent-husband. 
The parties have also decided to put an end to all other
litigations  pending  between  them  which  are  detailed  in
paragraph 9 of the Settlement Agreement which reads as under:
“9. That subsequently, the following civil/criminal cases
have been filed by the parties against each other:
(i) HMA Case No.1717 of 2022 filed under Section 13 of Hindu
Marriage Act titled as “Amitendra Singh Chauhan v. Gargi
Chauhan  &  Ors.”,  pending  before  the  Court  of  Addl.
Principal Judge, Family Court, District Gurugram (Haryana).
(ii) Case No.26/2022 under Guardian and Wards Act, 1890 titled
as “Amitendra Singh Chauhan v. Gargi Chauhan ” and pending
before the Ld. Principal Judge, Family Court, Dist. Gautam
Budh Nagar (U.P.)
(iii) Case No.1747/2021 lodged u/s 12 of the Protection of
Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 titled “ Gargi v.
Amitendra Singh Chauhan ” and pending before the Ld. Court
of Civil Judge (JD) /FTC Gautam Budh Nagar (U.P.)
(iv) Case No.261816/2022 under Sections 498 A, 323, 504, 506
IPC, PS-Women Police Station Noida, Gautam Budh Nagar, U.P.
pending before the Ld. Court of Civil Judge (JD)/FTC,
Gautam Budh Nagar, U.P. titled as “ State Government v.
Amitendra Singh Chauhan” in CC No.84/2021.
2

--- Page 306 ---
(v) Three Execution Petitions bearing Nos.916 of 2023, 848 of
2022 and 907 of 2022 in Maintenance Case No.214 of 2022 for
executing orders passed u/s 125(3) of the Cr.P.C. titled
“Ishani & Anr v. Amitendra Singh Chauhan ” pending before
the Ld. Principal Judge, Family Court, Dist. Gautam Budh
Nagar (U.P.)
(vi) Civil Suit (Filing no.415 of 2021) for cancellation of
Gift Deed to Gargi Chauhan titled ” Amitendra Singh Chauhan
v. Ramendra Kumar Singh & Ors. ” pending before the Ld.
Civil Judge, Junior Division Dist. Banda (U.P.)
(vii) Criminal Revision Petition No.32 of 2023 titled “ Ishani
and another v. State of U.P. & Anr. ” pending before the
Hon’ble High Court of Judicature at Allahabad.
(viii) Criminal Revision No.5228 of 2022 titled “Amitendra
Singh Chauhan v. State of U.P. & Ors.”, pending before the
Hon’ble High Court of Judicature at Allahabad.
(ix) Civil  Suit  No.220  of  2024,  titled  “Amitendra  Singh
Chauhan v. Gargi Chauhan”, pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Gautam Budh Nagar, U.P.
(x) Civil Suit No.1111 of 2024, titled “ Gargi Chauhan v.
Amitendra Singh Chauhan” , pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Gautam Budh Nagar, U.P.
Learned counsel for the respective parties, therefore,
submitted that the Settlement Agreement may be accepted and
appropriate orders may be made.
Learned counsel for the respective parties also submitted
that the parties have filed their independent applications  
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India seeking the very
same relief of dissolution of their marriage by a decree of
divorce by mutual consent on the terms and conditions stated in
the Settlement Agreement. The said applications may also be
3

--- Page 307 ---
allowed  and  consequently,  this  Transfer  Petition  may  be
disposed of accordingly.
The petitioner has appeared through video conferencing
facility while the respondent-husband is present in-person
before the Court. When we queried the parties about the
settlement terms and their decision to part ways, they stated
that they have indeed decided to resolve all their disputes by
seeking dissolution of their marriage by a decree of divorce by
mutual consent on certain terms and conditions which are
delineated in the Settlement Agreement. 
They also stated that they have filed their respective
applications under Article 142 of the Constitution of India
seeking divorce by mutual consent. 
They  further  stated  that  they  have  arrived  at  a
settlement on their own free volition without there being any
coercion or undue influence from any side. 
The parties also stated that they would abide by the
terms of the Settlement Agreement dated 24.04.2025. 
In the above backdrop, we have taken on record the
Settlement Agreement as well as the applications filed by the
parties under Article 142 of the Constitution of India.
4

--- Page 308 ---
It is noted that in total a sum of Rs.2,50,00,000/-
(Rupees Two Crores and Fifty lakhs Only) shall be paid by the
respondent-husband to the petitioner-wife in full and final
settlement of all claims of the petitioner and the minor
daughters. That the respondent shall have visitation  rights  as
indicated in the Settlement Agreement. That the parties have
also decided to seek dissolution of their marriage by a decree
of divorce by mutual consent. Consequently, the parties have
also sought for quashing of the aforesaid proceedings which
have been initiated by them against each other. All those cases
are quashed.
We take note of all these terms and conditions and the
plea made by the learned counsel for the parties in the
presence of the parties.
On perusal of the terms of the settlement agreement, we
find that the terms are lawful and there is no legal impediment
in accepting the same. Therefore, the  terms of the Settlement
Agreement are accepted by us. 
We  have  also  perused  the  applications  filed  by  the
parties under Article 142 of the Constitution of India seeking
dissolution of their marriage by a decree of divorce by mutual
consent. 
5

--- Page 309 ---
In view of the settlement arrived at between the parties,
we find no legal impediment in accepting their prayers seeking
dissolution of their marriage. Hence, the marriage between the
parties  which  was  solemnized  on  22.01.2006  at  Kanpur  is
dissolved by a decree of divorce by mutual consent. 
The terms of the settlement agreement dated 24.04.2025
shall form a part and parcel of this order.
The Registry is directed to draw-up a decree in the
aforesaid terms.
Consequently, this Transfer Petition stands disposed of
in the aforesaid terms.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
……………………………………………………………J.
                                     (B.V. NAGARATHNA)       
 
……………………………………………………………J.

1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
06.05.2026 in CRLM No.29258/2026 titled “ Sudhir Kumar & Anr.
vs. State of Bihar”  passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna.
3. On 27.05.2026, this Court passed the following order:-
“1. The petitioners challenges the judgment
and  order  dated  06-05-2026  in  CRLM  No.
29258/2026 titled “Sudhir Kumar & Anr. vs.
State of Bihar” passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Patna. 
2. Issue notice, returnable on 20.07.2026.
3. Mr. Manish Kumar, learned Advocate-on-
Record, accepts notice on behalf of the
State of Bihar. Hence, the formal service
of  notice  on  the  respondent-State  is
waived. Let notice be issued to respondent
No.2.
4.  Dasti  service,  in  addition,  is
permitted. Let steps for service be taken
within one week.
1

--- Page 311 ---
5.  In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be
mentioned that respondent is required to
file a counter affidavit and reply to the
interlocutory  application(s),  if  any,
before the next date of listing.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioners to
serve a copy of the present petition to the
learned  counsel  for  the  respondent-State
within a period of one week.
7. Let counter affidavit be filed within
two weeks. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be
filed within one week thereafter.
8. In the event of arrest in connection
with  F.I.R.  No.5117089260007  dated
14.01.2026,  registered  at  P.S.  Mahila
Thana,  District-Patna,  the  petitioners
shall  be  released  on  bail  by  the
Investigating/Arresting  Officer  on  such
terms and conditions as imposed and found
to be just, fair and reasonable.
9. However, in the attending facts, one of
the conditions shall necessarily be that if
the petitioners possess Passport, the same
shall  be  deposited  before  the  Competent
Authority/Court,  until  the  conclusion  of
the Trial or unless directed otherwise.
10. The petitioners shall make themselves
available before the Investigating Officer
on 03.06.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on all such
dates as they may be required.
11. Needless to add, till the investigation
is  not  complete  in  all  respects,  the
petitioners would fully cooperate, and if
the  challan/charge  sheet  is  filed,  they
would maintain good conduct and not attempt
to influence any of the witnesses in any
manner till the completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and also
perused the materials available on record.
2

--- Page 312 ---
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied  with;  the  appellants  have  cooperated  during  the
investigation; they have not threatened or intimidated any of
the witnesses; or have tried to influence the investigation in
any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order dated
27.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction to the
appellants  to  continue  to  cooperate  during  the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellants shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10 a.m.
and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is complete in
all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on account of the
appellants’ conduct, it shall be open to the Investigating
Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court to take appropriate steps
in accordance with the law, including cancellation of bail.
9. Further, if the appellants possess passports, the same
shall be surrendered before the Competent Authority/Court till
the conclusion of the trial or directed otherwise.

--- Page 313 ---
10. The impugned judgment and order dated 06.05.2026 is set

--- Page 335 ---
1
 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No(s).  1367/2011
SHANKARAPPA  (DEAD) & ORS.                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
EARAMMA                                            Respondent(s)
      O R D E R
1. The  present  appeal  arises  out  of  the
impugned judgment and decree dated 05.01.2010 passed
by the High Court of Karnataka Circuit Bench at
Gulbarga in RFA No.11 of 2000 whereby the appeal
preferred by respondent no.1/ plaintiff,  Earamma,
was allowed.
2. During pendency of this appeal, the parties
have entered into a settlement and recorded a deed
of settlement dated 11.07.2023. The same is annexed
to the application (IA No. 209193/2025) preferred by
the respondent with a prayer that the settlement be
taken on record and the appeal be disposed of in
terms of Settlement Agreement dated 11.07.2023  and
paragraph-6 of the application (IA No. 209193/2025).
3. During  pendency  of  the  present  appeal,  the
appellant  no.1,  Shankarappa, died  on  27.05.2019.
However, appellant nos.2 to 5, namely, Veerashetty,
Gurappa, Prakash and Venkat are the legal heirs of
the deceased appellant no.1,  Shankarappa. Appellant

--- Page 336 ---
2
no.3, Gurappa S/o. Shankarappa, died in August, 2023
after putting signatures on the deed of settlement
dated 11.07.2023.
4. Appellant no.4, Prakash S/o Gurappa, died on
31.12.2025 leaving his wife/ widow,  Nilambika and
two minor sons, Chirag and Siddharood.  Since, the
parties have already settled the matter, we direct
impleadment of Nilambika, Chirag and Siddarud as
legal heirs of appellant no.4,  Prakash.  Memo of
parties be corrected accordingly.
5. In the application (IA No. 209193/2025), the
respondent  would  state  that  in  the  Month  of
September, 2023 after the Settlement was recorded on
11.07.2023, the parties herein orally agreed to give
the entire 04 acres and 03 guntas situated in Survey
No.303 to the sole respondent herein along with 04
acres  in  Land  Survey  No.47,   both  situated  in
Sultanbad Village, Humnabad Taluk, Bidar District,
Karnataka in lieu of, and to satisfy allotment of 30
percent share to the respondent.
6. It is also mentioned in paragraph no.6 of the
application (IA No. 209193/2025) that the respondent
has already been given possession of above area in
Survey No.303 by the appellants herein.
7. The  contents  of  paragraph  no.6  of  the
application (IA No. 209193/2025) is not disputed by
Mr.  Basava  Prabhu  Patil,  learned  senior  counsel

--- Page 337 ---
3
appearing for the appellants and he agrees to the
disposal of the appeal in terms of the Settlement
read with the modification in paragraph no.6 of the
application (IA No. 209193/2025).

disposed of in terms of settlement dated 11.07.2023
between the parties with the modification mentioned
in  paragraph  no.6  of  the  application  (IA  No.
209193/2025) which is reproduced here under for ready

“6.  That subsequently, in the month of
September,  2023,  the  parties  herein
orally agreed to give the entire 4 Acres
and 03 Guntas situated in Sy. No.303,
Sultanabad,  Bidar,  Karnataka  to  the
Respondent herein/Eramma, along with 4
Acres  in  Sy.  No.47,  situated  at
Sultanabad.  It is humbly submitted that
the Respondent has been given possession
of Sy No.303 by the Appellants herein.”

dated 10.03.2026.

before the High Court, we may only observe that no

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
CIVIL APPEAL Diary No(s). 32327/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  27-03-2026
in CAAT(I) No. 1683/2025 passed by the National Company Law
Appellate Tribunal]
CONSORTIUM OF M/S CROWN STEELS AND M/S SUNRISE INDUSTRIES
Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
DELHI METRO RAIL CORPORATION LIMITED & ORS.        Respondent(s)
IA No. 173144/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 173147/2026 - EX-PARTE STAY
IA No. 173143/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s)  Dr. Abhishek Manu Singhvi, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Amish Tandon, Adv.
                   Mr. Ayush Beotra, Adv.
                   Ms. Vanshita Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Charchika Yadav, Adv.
                   Mr. Priyansha Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Sameer Abhyankar, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s)  Mr Tarun Johri, AOR
    Mr Ankur Gupta, Adv.
Mr Siddharth Praveen Acharya, Adv.
Mr Princess Sigh, Adv.
Ms Sapna Bharadwaj, Adv. 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Appellant  is  the  successful  resolution
1

--- Page 351 ---
applicant. The submission on behalf of the appellant
is that under the Resolution Plan, in paragraphs
10.9 to 10.11, DMRC/Delhi Development Authority/MCD/
Any Departments/Authorities were to allow the change
in management and shareholding of the PIPL/Corporate
Debtor  and  were  to  provide  other  required
permissions for continuation of the existing rights,
lease, licenses etc. to the new management on the
basis  of  Resolution  plan  approved  by  the
Adjudicating Authority. Further, Delhi Metro Rail
Corporation  (“DMRC”)  was  required  to  revive  the
Concession  Agreement  in  favour  of  the  Corporate
Debtor as assigned by Pratibha Industries Limited
(“PIL”) in favour of the Corporate Debtor in full
force and effect without any additional liability
towards the Corporate Debtor/ Resolution Applicant.
3. It is the case of the appellant that the order
of the National Company Law Tribunal (“Adjudicating
Authority”) dated 30.07.2024 approved the Resolution
Plan. Paragraph 10.2 of the approval order stated
that the Resolution Plan shall be binding on the
Corporate Debtor, its employees, members, creditors,
Central Government, State Government or any local
authority to whom a debt in respect of the payment
of dues arising under any law for the time being in
force  is  due,  guarantors  and  other  stakeholders
involved in the Resolution Plan, so that the revival
2

--- Page 352 ---
of  the  Corporate  Debtor  comes  under  force  with
immediate effect.
4. It is also the case of the appellant that the
approval  order  dated  30.07.2024  passed  by  the
Adjudicating Authority approving the Resolution Plan
has attained finality for want of challenge by any
stakeholder  and,  therefore,  DMRC,  which  is  a
stakeholder, is bound by the approved Resolution
Plan.
5. The record reflects that the stand of DMRC was
that the Concession Agreement was terminated prior
to  initiation  of  Corporate  Insolvency  Resolution
Process  (“CIRP”).  Therefore,  the  rights  of  the
parties  qua  the subject matter of the Concession
Agreement stood crystallized prior to the initiation
of CIRP and those rights cannot be affected by the
Resolution Plan.
6. At this stage, the submission on behalf of the
appellant  is  that  the  Successful  Resolution
Applicant on approval of the plan steps into the
shoes of the Corporate Debtor and can take recourse
to  all  the  remedies  that  would  be  otherwise
available  to  the  Corporate  Debtor  against  the
termination of the Concession Agreement. He further
submits  that  already  arbitration  clause  stood
invoked and, therefore, the validity of termination
of  the  Concession  Agreement  would  have  to  be
3

--- Page 353 ---
determined in terms thereof.
7. It is also the case of the appellant that vide
letter dated 24.04.2026, DMRC has taken a stand that
Concession  Agreement,  under  which  PIL/PIPL  were
granted rights for development of the project, stood
terminated on 01.03.2017 and cannot be restored.
However, in the very same letter, it is also stated
that  DMRC  is  ready  to  consider  conciliation  /
arbitration in accordance with law and, hence, Prime
Infra Park may invoke conciliation by submitting a
proper formal request.
8. In that context, the submission on behalf of the
appellant is that once arbitration clause stands
invoked, and there is nothing in the resolution plan
extinguishing  such  proceeding,  the  dispute  would
have  to  be  resolved  through  arbitration/
conciliation, but paragraph 14(i) of the impugned
order may come in the way of resolution of the
dispute as it holds that no direction can be issued
to the appellant to revive the Concession Agreement
terminated on 01.03.2017.
9. In the above backdrop, we deem it appropriate to
require the respondents to file their affidavit to
ascertain their stand as to whether they are willing
to  undertake  the  arbitration  /conciliation
proceedings qua revival of the Concession Agreement.
10. Issue notice, returnable on 12.08.2026.
4

--- Page 354 ---
11. Notice on behalf of the respondents has been
accepted by Mr. Tarun Johri.
12. In the meantime, the petitioner would not be
required to make deposit of any further amount under
the Resolution Plan.                       
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
5

--- Page 355 ---

1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
19.02.2026 in CRMM No. 54602/2025(O&M) titled “ Sarabjeet Kaur
vs. State of Punjab ” passed by the High Court of Punjab &
Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. On 22.05.2026, this Court passed the following order:-
“1. The petitioner challenges the judgment
and  order  dated  19.02.2026  in  CRM-M
No.54602/2025  (O&M)  passed  by  the  High
Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh. 
2. We have heard learned counsel for the
petitioner.
3. Issue notice, returnable on 20.07.2026.
4.  Mr.  Karan  Sharma,  learned  counsel,
accepts  notice  on  behalf  of  the
respondent/State. Hence, the formal service
of notice on the respondent is waived.
5. The petitioner to serve a copy of the
present petition 1 to learned counsel for
the respondent forthwith.
6. Let counter affidavit be filed within
three  (03)  weeks.  Rejoinder  affidavit
thereto, if any, be filed before the next
date of listing.
7.  We  make  it  clear  that  no  further
1                          

--- Page 364 ---
opportunity  will  be  granted  for  this
purpose.
8. In the event of arrest in connection
with  Crime  No.107  of  2024  of  Sadar
Kotkapura  Police  Station,  Faridkot
District, Punjab, the petitioner shall be
released on bail on the appropriate terms
and  conditions  as  may  be  fixed  by  the
Investigating  Officer/Arresting  Officer.
However, in the attending facts, one of the
conditions shall necessarily be that if the
petitioner possesses a passport, the same
shall  be  deposited  before  the  Competent
Authority/Court, till the conclusion of the
Trial or directed otherwise.
9.  The  petitioner  shall  make  himself
available before the Investigating Officer
on 29.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on all such
dates as he may be required.
10. Needless to add, till the investigation
is  not  complete  in  all  respects,  the
petitioner(s) would fully cooperate, and if
the challan/charge sheet is filed, he would
maintain good conduct and not attempt to
influence  any  of  the  witnesses  in  any
manner till the completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and also
perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied  with;  the  appellant  has  cooperated  during  the
investigation; she has not threatened or intimidated any of the
witnesses; or has tried to influence the investigation in any
manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order dated
2                          

--- Page 365 ---
22.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction to the
appellant  to  continue  to  cooperate  during  the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary

Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10 a.m.
and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is complete in
all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on account of the
appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to the  Investigating
Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court to take appropriate steps
in accordance with the law, including cancellation of bail.
9. Further, if the appellant possesses a passport, the same
shall be surrendered before the Competent Authority/Court till
the conclusion of the trial or directed otherwise.
10. The impugned judgment and order dated 19.02.2026 is set
aside.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                 O R D E R
1. The present petition arises from the impugned
order dated 20.05.2026 passed by the High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in CRM-M No.27368
of  2026  whereby  the  second  regular  bail
application filed by the petitioner was rejected
by the High Court.
2. The petitioner was accused in FIR No.07 of
2023 dated 11.01.2023 under Sections 419, 420,
465, 467, 468, 471 and 120-B of the Indian Penal
Code (for short, “the IPC”) registered at Police
Station City Zira, District Ferozepur.
3. The petitioner had been in custody since
21.08.2025.  By  order  dated  16.06.2026,  a  co-
ordinate bench of this Court had issued notice.
4. Heard Sri Gaurav Vir Singh Behl, learned
counsel appearing for the petitioner and Sri Vivek
Jain,  learned  Additional  Attorney  General
appearing for the respondent – State of Punjab.
5. The petitioner has been in jail for 10 months
SLP (Crl.) No.11264/2026 2/4

--- Page 377 ---
and the allegation is of a forged sale deed having
been registered, after the death of the vendor.
The petitioner attested as a witness and hence has
been roped in. The other co-accused who were
alleged  with  the  crime  of  forgery  have  been
granted bail. The petitioner is implicated for the
offence by virtue of Section 120B.
6. On perusal of the material on record and
after hearing the learned counsel for the parties,
we  are  of  the  prima  facie opinion  that  the
petitioner can be released on bail, subject to
cooperation with the investigation and such other
terms  and  conditions  that  the  Sub-Divisional
Judicial  Magistrate,  Zira  (the  jurisdictional
court) may deem fit and appropriate to impose. The
petitioner shall appear before the I.O. as and
when  directed  and  shall  also  on  specific
directions issued appear before the jurisdictional
court.
7. With the above directions, the Special Leave
Petition is disposed of.

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN

SLP (Crl.) No.8499/2026 1/4

--- Page 380 ---
  UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                     O R D E R
1. The present petition arises from the impugned
order dated 28.03.2026 passed by the High Court of
Rajasthan  at  Jodhpur  in  S.B.  Criminal
Miscellaneous  Bail  Application  No.12133/2025
whereby the fourth regular bail application filed
by the petitioner was rejected by the High Court.
2. The petitioner was accused in FIR No.93 of
2020 dated 16.08.2020 under Sections 147, 148,
149, 302, 307, 394, 365 of the Indian Penal Code
(for short, “the IPC”) and under Sections 3 and 25
of the Arms Act, 1959 registered at Rageshwari Gas
Terminal Police Station, District Barmer.
3. Heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner and the learned counsel appearing for
the respondent – State of Rajasthan.
4. The petitioner had been in custody since
17.08.2020 until 14.05.2026 when we released the
petitioner on interim bail, in addition to issuing
notice; by order dated 14.05.2026. In the same
SLP (Crl.) No.8499/2026 2/4

--- Page 381 ---
order, we had noted that all the co-accused had
been released on bail.
5. On the last date, the State counsel had
brought to our notice that though the trial was
expedited, the petitioner filed a Crl. M. C.
before the High Court in which the trial was
stayed. The challenge was also on the trial court
having framed charge for the offence of murder,
while the petitioner’s case is that he ought to
have been charged under Section 304 of the IPC.
6. By order dated 13.07.2026 after noticing the
above, we gave an option to the petitioner to
withdraw the Crl. M.C. so that the trial can be
expedited, since the mere framing of the charge
does not prejudice the petitioner in the trial.
7. Today we are informed that the petitioner has
withdrawn the Crl. M.C. filed before the High
Court and it is submitted by the learned State
Counsel; on a specific query, that the material
witnesses have been examined.
SLP (Crl.) No.8499/2026 3/4

--- Page 382 ---
8. In the above circumstances, considering the
fact that the petitioner has been in jail for
almost six years, and all the co-accused have been
released, we are of the prima facie opinion that
the petitioner can be released on bail subject to
cooperation in the trial and on such terms and
conditions as the Additional District and Sessions
Judge, Balotra shall deem fit to impose.
9. With the above directions, the Special Leave
Petition is disposed of.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also
stand disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                  (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)              COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP (Crl.) No.8499/2026 4/4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.8996 of 2026)
  PINTU KUMAR @ PINTU SINGH                  APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
  THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
09.02.2026 in Criminal Appeal (SJ) No.2680 of 2025,
titled “Pintu Kumar @ Pintu Singh Vs. The State of Bihar
& Anr.”, passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.
3. On  15.05.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the  judgment  and  order
dated 09-02-2026 in CRASJ No. 2680/2025 titled “Pintu
Kumar @ Pintu Singh vs. State of Bihar & Anr.” passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Patna. 
2. Issue notice, returnable on 14.07.2026.
3.  Mr.  Azmat  Hayat  Amanullah,  learned  Advocate-on-
Record accepts notice on behalf of the State of Bihar.
Hence, the formal service of notice on the respondent-
State is waived. Let notice be issued to respondent No.2.
4. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
service be taken within one week.
5. In the notice itself, let it be mentioned that respondent
1

--- Page 388 ---
is required  to file a counter affidavit  and reply to the
interlocutory application(s), if any, before the next date of
listing.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner to serve a copy of
the  present  petition  to  the  learned  counsel  for  the
respondentState within a period of one week.
7.  Let  counter  affidavit  be  filed  within  two  weeks.
Rejoinder  thereto,  if  any,  be  filed  within  one  week
thereafter.
8.  In  the  event  of  arrest  in  connection  with  F.I.R.
No.96/2025 dated 10.05.2025, registered at P.S.Meskaur,
Nawada, Bihar, the petitioner shall be released on bail by
the  Investigating/Arresting  Officer  on  such  terms  and
conditions  as  imposed  and  found  to  be  just,  fair  and
reasonable.
9. However, in the attending facts, one of the conditions
shall  necessarily  be  that  if  the  petitioner  possesses  a
Passport,  the  same  shall  be  deposited  before  the
Competent Authority/Court, until the conclusion of the
Trial or unless directed otherwise.
10. The petitioner shall make himself available before the
Investigating Officer on 22.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on
all such dates as he may be required.
11. Needless to add, till the investigation is complete in
all respects, the petitioner would fully cooperate, and if
the challan/charge sheet is filed, he would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any of the witnesses
in any manner till the completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
2

--- Page 389 ---
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 15.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  seek  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 09.02.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
        ......................J.
            (SANJAY KAROL)
                            ........................J.
                               (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)     
NEW DELHI;
JULY 21, 2026.
3

                 RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order
