AWADHESH KUMAR MISHRA                Petitioner(s)
                           VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS.        Respondent(s)
IA No. 177800/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN
FILING
IA No. 177801/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF 
THE IMPUGNED JUDGMENT
IA No. 177803/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 177802/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE LENGTHY 
LIST OF DATES
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Deeksha Dwivedi, Adv.
                   Mr. Paras Nath Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Fahad M. Khan, Adv.
                   Mr. Nilesh Jain, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Adarsh Upadhyay, AOR
                   Ms. Pallavi Kumari, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashank Pachauri, Adv.   
1

--- Page 400 ---
     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the 
following
                      O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Having regard to the fact that the petitioner
is in custody and the learned counsel appearing
for the petitioner is arguing as a  pro bono
counsel, we deem it proper to condone the delay
though being inordinate as no litigant would stand
to benefit by approaching the Court belatedly.
Hence, keeping in mind the principles laid down by
this Court in Collector Land Acquisition, Anantnag
& Anr. Vs. Mst. Katiji & Ors.,  reported in AIR
1987 SC 1353, we condone the delay.
3. Having regard to the fact that the petitioner
is in custody for 16 years 09 months and 13 days
(without remission) and 19 years 05 months 15 days
(with remission) and this factor sways our mind to
suspend the sentence imposed on the petitioner and
grant him bail on such terms as the jurisdictional
Sessions  Court  may  deem  fit  to  impose.
Accordingly, we direct that the petitioner be
2

--- Page 401 ---
released on bail by the jurisdictional Sessions
Courts on such terms and conditions as it deems
fit to impose. 
4.  The aforesaid order has been passed by this
Court  taking  into  consideration  not  only  the
number of years petitioner being incarcerated but
also the fact that he has not been let out even
for a single day on parole.
5. Be that as it may, the issue relating to the
weapon which is said to have been used by the
petitioner for shooting the victim – Manoj Kumar
has been seized but not sent to the FSL or in
other  words,  prima  facie,  there  is  lack  of
ballistic evidence. In fact, the High Court itself
disbelieved the recovery of the weapon. As such we
are of the considered view that suspicion cannot
substitute to proof and the fact that the co-
accused, namely, the alleged instigators having
been acquitted, the motive / common intention
alleged  against  petitioner  will  have  to  be
examined with great caution.
3

--- Page 402 ---
6. For the above-stated cumulative reasons, the
order of suspension of sentence and grant of bail
has been passed by this Court.
7. Counter-affidavit, if any, shall be filed
within four weeks from today, if not already filed
and pendency of the present petition would not
come in the way of consideration of the remission
application  said  to  have  been  filed  by  the
petitioner and the authorities shall consider the
same on its own merits and in accordance with law
and place the decision taken thereon on the file
of this case.
8. Re-list on 22.09.2026. 
  (NEHA GUPTA)                      (AVGV RAMU)

[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 23-06-2025 in ARB
No. 15617/2025 passed by the High  Court  of  Judicature at Bombay]
WALLOP ADVERTISING                                 Petitioner(s)
VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ANR.                              Respondent(s)
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) :
Mr. Mohammed Zain Khan, Adv.
Mr. Ashraf Kapoor, Adv.

For Respondent(s) :
     Mr. Rishabh Sahu, Adv.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Mohammed  Zain  Khan,  learned  counsel
appearing  for  the  petitioner  and  Mr.  Rishabh  Sahu,
learned counsel appearing for the respondents.
2. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
question the correctness of the Order dated 23rd June,
2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay in
1

--- Page 404 ---
Arbitration Petition (L) No.15617 of 2025, by which the
High Court has refused to extend the time under Section
29A of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (for
short, “the Act” ) and has dismissed the application for
extension. The High Court has recorded that the mandate
of the Arbitral Tribunal expired way back in February,
2020 and the extension application being filed five years
later cannot be entertained.  
3. By our Order dated 25.02.2026, we had called for a
Report from the Arbitrator, as regards the status of the
arbitration. 
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the
parties  and  perused  the  relevant  material  placed  on
record. We have also gone through the report of the
Arbitrator. 
5. The  report  indicates  that  after  the  Arbitrator
entered appearance in 2018, a series of applications
under Section 17 of the Act were heard till January,
2019. Thereafter, during 2019 and early 2020, proceedings
continued with disposal of other applications, framing of
issues and so on. The Arbitrator has stated that though
the  mandate  expired  on  1st  February,  2020,  he  was
informed that an application under Section 29-A of the
Act was filed and pending and both the parties agreed  to
continue with the proceedings.
2

--- Page 405 ---
6. According to the petitioner herein, an application
during the period of Covid-19 pandemic was filed by e-
mail on 06.07.2020 in accordance with the prevailing
protocol. It is stated under the bona fide belief that
the application was pending, the proceedings continued.
Upon  discovering  that  the  earlier  e-mail  filed
application was not reflected on the Registry record, the
petitioner filed a formal application on 13.05.2025. We
have  perused the  covering letter  to the  email. The
Petitioner also avers about sending email on 06.07.2020
in the application filed on 13.05.2025. In the Counter
affidavit filed in this Court the respondents admits
receiving the copy of the said email. 
7. Today we are informed that the arbitration is at an
advanced stage and if a six months period is given, the
award itself can be pronounced.
8. Having  considered  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances  and  after  perusing  the  report  of  the
Arbitrator and in the peculiar facts of the case, we
direct  that  the  time  for  concluding  the  arbitral
proceedings and pronouncing the award is extended till
31st January, 2027.
9. At this stage, Mr. Rishabh Sahu, learned counsel
for the respondent submits that the petitioner has moved
a Writ Petition in the High Court challenging the orders
of the Arbitrator holding certain claims to be non-
arbitrable.
3

--- Page 406 ---
10. Mr. Mohammed Zain Khan, learned counsel for the
petitioner, fairly submits that the petitioner will not
press the Writ Petition so that there is no impediment
for the Arbitrator to comply with the directions made by
this Court in the present matter
11. With  the  above  observations,  the  Special  Leave
Petition is disposed of.
12. Pending  application,  if  any,  shall  also  stand
disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER

--- Page 407 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.    /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.16063/2025
ANILBHAI NANUHAI CHAVDA  APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT & ANR. RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant, who is not an accused, sought for release of the
vehicle having Registration No.GJ-13-AX-8885 which carried 15
animals in inhuman condition. Hence, the vehicle came to be seized
along with another vehicle. An application was filed by the
appellant for release of the said vehicle, claiming to be the
registered owner of the same and same was not entertained by both
the courts and having suffered an order of rejection the appellant
is now before this court.
4. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the appellant
who  has  prayed  for  release  of  the  vehicle  contending  that
appellant is ready and willing to abide by such conditions as may
be imposed by this court or by the trial court, he seeks for
release of the vehicle.
1

--- Page 408 ---
5. Per contra , learned counsel appearing for the respondent-
State has vehemently argued and contended that the vehicle was
illegally used for transporting animals from Gujarat to Kerala and
if the said vehicle is released there is likelihood of the said
vehicle being used for similar criminal activity and despite there
being no pass or permit to transport the animals, the same have
been used for transporting and as such the vehicle should not be
released. Hence, she has prayed for dismissal of the appeal.
6. Having heard the learned counsels appearing for the parties
and on perusal of the records, we notice that there is no dispute
with regard to the ownership of the vehicle having vested with the
appellant. To allay the apprehension of the respondent-prosecution
it would suffice if stringent conditions are imposed for the
release of the vehicle and also taking into consideration the
stationing of the said vehicle, without being used, would in all
probability result in said vehicle getting rusted and is likely to
become unusable. Hence, we are of the considered view that prayer
sought for deserves to be granted.
7. Hence, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set aside. The
vehicle in question namely GJ-13-AX-8885 is ordered to be released
in favour of appellant on such terms and conditions including the
conditions of directing the appellant to maintain the vehicle as
it is and produce the same before the jurisdictional Investigation
Officer (IO) at least once in fifteen days and not to sell,
encumber or part with the said vehicle. The trial court would also
be at liberty to impose such other further conditions as it may
deem fit.
2

--- Page 409 ---
8. With these conditions, the appeal stands allowed.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.................J.
  (ARAVIND KUMAR)
.................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.1172/2012
CHEIF GENERA MANAGER, BSNL  APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
COLLECTOR OF CENTRAL EXCISE, BHOPAL RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. The issues involved in this appeal relates to classification
of the product steel tubular transmission poles (Hamilton poles or
tubes) manufactured by Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited (for short
“BSNL”) for the purpose of levy of Central Excise Duty, namely,
whether  the  goods  are  classifiable  under  heading  7308.90
(structures / parts of structures, eligible for exemption) or
under heading 7306.90 (tubes and pipes). Same is no more  res
integra in view of the law laid down by this Court in the matter
of India Metals and Ferro Alloys Ltd., Cuttack Vs. Collector of
Central Excise, Bhubaneswar, 1991 Supp (1) SCC 125.
2. However, the learned counsel appearing for the appellant, by
drawing our attention to the circular No.A-28/1990 dated 27.09.1990
at Annexure-A1 would contend that the goods in question was
classified under sub-heading 7308.90, which was later changed by
circular  dated  06.09.2000  vide  Circular  No.545/41/2000-CX  at
Annexure-A5. As such, the levy for the month of July and August,
2000  would  amount  to  retrospective  application,  which  is
impermissible, and this fact has not been considered by the
1

--- Page 412 ---
authorities as well as by the Appellate Tribunal, namely, CESTAT.
Hence, the learned counsel appearing for the appellant has prayed
for same being set aside and levy being quashed, at least for the
months of July and August, 2000.
3. Per contra , Mr.  Raghavendra P Shankar , learned Additional
Solicitor General of India appearing for the respondent, would
support the impugned order and contends that the show cause notice
itself is of the year 2001, and by circular dated 06.09.2000, the
department has only reiterated the law laid down by this Court in
India Metals and Ferro Alloys Ltd., Cuttack  referred to supra.
Hence, the question of retrospectivity would not arise.
4. Having heard the learned advocates appearing for the parties,
and on perusal of the records, we are of the considered view that
the contention raised by the learned counsel appearing for the
appellant does not hold water.
5. A perusal of the show cause notice dated 25.07.2001 would
clearly indicate that it is issued subsequent to the circular dated
06.09.2000, and even in the said circular, what has been stated is
reiteration of the law laid down by this Court, namely, the goods
in question is classifiable under the sub-heading 7306.90 and not
under  7308.90.  As  such,  the  question  of  the  levy  being
retrospectively being applied does not arise.
6. Even otherwise also, it can be noticed that this Court in the
above-referred judgment has clearly held that the goods in question
is classifiable under the sub-heading 7306.90, which judgment came
to be rendered on 22.11.1990 and as such, the show cause notice
issued on 25.07.2001 relating to levy for the period of July and
2

--- Page 413 ---
August, 2000 cannot be construed or held as being retrospectively.
In that view of the matter also, we are of the considered view that
there is no merit in this appeal. Accordingly, it stands dismissed.
7. Pending applications, if any, stands disposed of. Costs made
easy.
.................J.
  (ARAVIND KUMAR)
.................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.
3

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        /2026
       [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.6547/2026]
MAHARAJ SARAN                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ORS.                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The present appeal has been filed against the
impugned order dated 19.12.2024 passed by the High
Court, wherein a writ petition was filed by the
appellant seeking a writ of habeas corpus to release
his son, i.e., the detenu.   The detenu was detained
at the airport pursuant to a Look Out circular
issued against him on 13.12.2024 in FIR No.07/2024
registered  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections  66,  72  and  84-C  of  the  Information
Technology Act, 2000 (for short, ‘the IT Act’) and
Section 78(1)(ii) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita,
2023 registered on 07.11.2024 at P.S. Cyber Crime,
District – Fazilka, Punjab.
The High Court, while disposing of the writ petition
had observed that ex-facie, Section 66 of the IT Act
has not been defined as a bailable offence under the
IT Act.  The said observation has been impugned by

--- Page 420 ---
2
the appellant before this Court.
We had issued a limited notice in the present
matter with respect to the legal issue involving
Section 66 of the IT Act, which has been treated as
a non-bailable offence by the High Court.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the parties.
In our considered view, the High Court did not
take into consideration Section 77B of the IT Act,
which has to be read to ascertain the nature of
offence provided under Section 66 of the IT Act.
In such view of the matter, the view expressed
by High Court that Section 66 of the IT Act has not
been defined as a bailable offence under the IT Act,
stands set aside.
The  appeal  stands  disposed  of,  with  the
aforesaid clarification.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of. 
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.

--- Page 431 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO(S).  2202/2023
A. SURENDER                                        Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF TELANGANA                             Respondent(s)
ORDER
 We  have  heard  the  learned  Senior  counsel
appearing for the appellant and the learned counsel
appearing for the respondent.
The appellant has been convicted by the Trial
Court for the offences punishable under Sections 13(1)
(a) and 13(1)(d), read with Section 13(2) of the
Prevention  of  Corruption  Act,  1988  (hereinafter
referred to as the “PC Act”) and sentenced to undergo
one year of rigorous imprisonment.  The High Court has
confirmed  the  same,  vide  the  impugned  order.
Aggrieved, the appellant is before us. 
The  sum  and  substance  of  the  case  of  the
prosecution against the appellant is that he being a
Motor Vehicle Inspector connived with accused No.2
(since deceased) and received a certain amount of
money, while discharging his public duty. 
Learned Senior counsel appearing for the appellant
submits that the evidence of PW3 has to be eschewed as
the same has not been put to the appellant by the
1

--- Page 432 ---
Trial court. What remains are the documents endorsing
the recording by accused No.2 of the words ‘West Zone’
and the evidence of PW2. Even from the above, the
complicity  of  the  appellant  cannot  be  proved.
Additionally, the learned Senior counsel appearing for
the appellant submitted that the demand has not been
proved and that there is no evidence to attract the
provisions of Sections 13(1)(a) and 13(1)(d) of the PC
Act to the effect that the appellant was actually
involved. Thus, both the Courts have committed an
error in convicting the appellant.
Learned counsel appearing for the State submits
that  both  the  Courts  have  rightly  considered  the
relevant materials, with specific reference to the
endorsement  made  by  accused  No.2.  The  fact  that
accused No.2 had died pending the appeal before the
High Court, would not enure to the benefit of the
appellant. PW2 has also clearly deposed in line with
the statement made under Section 164 of the Code of
Criminal Procedure, 1973. Both the Courts have read
the evidence of PW2 correctly. In such view of the
matter,  there  is  no  need  for  re-appreciating  the
evidence at this stage.
Upon  hearing  the  learned  Senior  counsel
appearing for the appellant and the learned counsel
appearing for the respondent, we are not inclined to
interfere with the ultimate conclusion arrived at by
2

--- Page 433 ---
both the Courts as regards the conviction. There is a
finding  of  both  the  Courts  with  respect  to  the
endorsement on the part of accused No.2.  PW2 has also
implicated accused No.2 and further deposed that the
money collected by accused No.2 was meant for accused
No.1, namely, the appellant.
In such view of the matter, we do not find any
perversity  warranting  interference  insofar  as  the
conviction is concerned.  However, taking note of the
fact that the alleged occurrence is of the year 2004,
and more than two decades have elapsed since then,
coupled with the fact that the appellant has already
undergone four months out of the one year sentence
imposed on him, we are inclined to modify the sentence
of the appellant to the one already undergone.
Accordingly,  the  sentence  of  the  appellant
stands modified to the one already undergone by him.
The appeal is allowed in part.
His bail bonds stand discharged.
Pending  application(s),  if  any  shall  stand
disposed of.
………………………………………..J.
                                                [M.M. SUNDRESH]
………………………………………..J.
                                               [PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
15.07.2026
3

ITEM NO.21               COURT NO.11               SECTION XVI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Special Leave Petition (CIVIL) Diary No(S). 37749/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  10-02-2026
in WPST No. 101/2025 and order dated 10-02-2026 in WPST No. 81/2025
passed by the High Court at Calcutta]
USHASI GHOSH CHAUDHURY                             Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF WEST BENGAL & ORS.                    Respondent(s)
IA No. 195444/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 195443/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vishal Prasad, AOR
                   Ms. Ritika Sethi, Adv.
                   Mr. Prem Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Soumya Ganguly, Adv.
                   Ms. Simontini Bhadra, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
3. This petition seeks to impugn judgment and order of the High
Court at Calcutta dated 10.02.2026 passed in W.P.S.T. No.101
of 2025 connected with W.P.S.T. No.81 of 2025.
4. The petitioner had applied for appointment on the post of
1

--- Page 440 ---
Senior Ayurvedic Medical Officer (SAMO) in the West Bengal
Ayurvedic Health Service under the Health & Family Welfare
Department, Government of West Bengal.  One of the prescribed
eligibility qualifications was that the candidate must be
registered either in Part A of the State Register of Ayurvedic
Medical Practitioners maintained by the Paschim Banga Ayurved
Parishad  (PBAP),  or  in  Part  I  of  the  Central  Register
maintained by the then Central Council of Indian Medicine.
The recruitment notification further requires the candidates
to fill up the requisite fields in the form relating to the
eligibility qualifications which they possess, and documents
in respect thereof were to be uploaded by the last date of
submission of application.
5. It is not in dispute that the petitioner had professed herself
to be registered with the State Register of Ayurvedic Medical
Practitioners. Notably, in her application she did not claim
herself to be registered in the Central Register.
6. Later, when her eligibility qualifications were verified, it
was found that her State Registration stood cancelled on
14.07.2023.  Since the registration professed by her in the
application was with the State Register and the same stood
cancelled, she was declared ineligible.
7. Impugning the rejection of her candidature, she filed an
original application before the West Bengal Administrative
Tribunal (Tribunal).  The Tribunal by relying upon the Central
Registration of the appellant, allowed the application.  
2

--- Page 441 ---
8. Aggrieved therewith, the State preferred writ petition before
the High Court.
9. The High Court found that admittedly the petitioner had
claimed her registration with the State Council to profess
herself eligible to submit her application for participation
in the recruitment process, therefore, verification had to be
in respect of that qualification. Since the qualification set
up in the application was registration with the State Council
which admittedly stood cancelled, and, in any event, was
liable to be cancelled in terms of “Practitioners of Indian
Medicine (Standards of Professional Conduct, Etiquette and
Code of Ethics) Regulations, 1982 as there could be no dual
registration  i.e.,  registration  with  two  or  more  State
Councils, the registration with the State Council, set up to
gain eligibility, was invalid. In such circumstances, the High
Court took the view that once the eligibility qualification
set up by the petitioner was found invalid, it was not open
for the petitioner to rely upon an alternative qualification
which was not set up in the application form.
10. The learned counsel for the petitioner submits that since the
eligibility qualification was either /or of the two i.e.
either the State Council or the Central Council, and there is
no lis that the petitioner possesses a valid registration of
the Central Council, there was no justification to hold that
the petitioner was not eligible.  
11. The submission of the learned counsel for the petitioner
3

--- Page 442 ---
though appears attractive, in our view, is not sustainable in
terms  of  the  recruitment  notice.  The  recruitment  notice
specifically states that a candidate would have to fill up the
details of the qualification that he possesses and documents
of those qualifications would have to be submitted by the last
date of submission of the application. In such circumstances,
the qualifications submitted by the last date of submission of
the  application  alone  could  be  considered  and  not  the
alternate  qualifications  which  were  not  set  up  in  the
application form by the last date of its submission. 
12. In our view, therefore, the High Court did not commit any
error in setting aside the order passed by the Tribunal.
Consequently, we find no merit in this petition.  The same is
accordingly dismissed.
13. All pending applications shall stand disposed of.
  (KAVITA PAHUJA)                              (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 443 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP(C) No. 24552/2025
Respondent no.1(Caveator) has already filed counter affidavit.
Opportunity of respondent no.2 to file counter affidavit has
already  been  declined  vide  Registrar  Court's  order  dated
13.04.2026.
Registry to process the matter for listing before the Hon’ble
Court as per rules.
SLP(C) No. 24888-24889/2025

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.10274/2026]
RAMPAL                               APPELLANT(S)
         
                   VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ORS.                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
01.05.2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature
at Allahabad rejecting the regular bail prayer of
the appellant in connection with Case Crime No.237
of  2019  registered  at  Police  Station  Baar,
District Lalitpur (U.P.).
4. At the outset, the learned counsel for the
appellant submits that the appellant has suffered
pending trial incarceration since 12.12.2019, and
the trial is still not over.  It has also been
submitted that there were multiple accused.  One
of the co-accused is Rajendra.  The victim is now
married to Rajendra.  In such circumstances, there
is no justification for the appellant to suffer
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.10274/2026                                                      1

--- Page 448 ---
pending trial incarceration.
5. The learned counsel for the State has opposed
the bail but could not dispute that the appellant
has suffered pending trial incarceration since
12.12.2019.
6. Having  regard  to  the  above,  without
expressing any opinion on merits of the case, we
are of the view that the appellant is entitled to
be released on bail, at this stage.
7. The appeal, is accordingly, allowed.  The
impugned order passed by the High Court rejecting
the bail prayer of the appellant stands set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court may
deem fit to impose in the facts and circumstances
of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………J
                        [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………J
                        [VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi
July 20, 2026
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.10274/2026                                                      2

ITEM NO.40               COURT NO.11               SECTION IV-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No(s). 14128-14129/2017
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  21-04-2016
in FAO No. 6121/2015 29-11-2016 in RA No. 140/2016 29-11-2016 in
FAO No. 6121/2015 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana at
Chandigarh]
PUNJAB AGRO FOOD GRAIN CORPORATION LTD. AND ANR.   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
GARG RICE MILLS THROUGH ITS PROPRIETOR SH. BARINDER KUMAR(DEAD) 
THROUGH LRS. Respondent(s)
 
Date : 17-07-2026 These petitions were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Venkateswara Rao Anumolu, AOR
                   Mr. Sushil Tekriwal, Adv.
                   Dr. Mamta Tekriwal, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the petitioner(s).
2. Through these petition(s), the petitioner(s)
seek leave to appeal against the order of the
High  Court 1 dated  29.11.2016  in  Review
Application No. 140-CII-2016 (O&M) in FAO No.
6121 of 2015.
1 High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh
1

--- Page 456 ---
3. An Arbitral Award was passed in favour of
the  petitioner(s).  Objections  to  the  award,
under  Section  34  of  the  Arbitration  and
Conciliation  Act,  19962,  were  filed  by  the
respondent(s). Those  objections were  rejected
vide order dated 22.12.2014.
4. Aggrieved by rejection of their objections,
an appeal under Section 37 of the 1996 Act was
preferred before the High Court. The High Court,
upon finding that the Award was a non-speaking
Award, allowed the appeal and set aside the
Award while observing that parties would have
liberty to resort to the provisions of Section
11(6) of the 1996 Act for appointment of a fresh
arbitrator. However, at that stage, the High
Court deemed it appropriate to itself appoint
Justice  Satish  Kumar  Mittal,  former  Chief
Justice  of  the  Rajashtan  High  Court,  as  an
Arbitrator. However,  Justice Mittal  expressed
his inability to continue with the arbitration
on account of his appointment as Lokayukta.
5. At that stage, the petitioner(s) filed a
review application seeking recall of the order
2 “1996 Act”
2

--- Page 457 ---
dated 21.04.2016 and dismissal of the appeal.
6. By the impugned order dated 29.11.2016, the
earlier order dated 21.04.2016 was recalled and
the appeal was decided on merits and the Award
was set aside, inter alia, for the same reason
i.e., lacking reasons.
7. The grievance of the petitioner(s) is that
if the Award is set aside on grounds that it
lacks  reasons,  the  claim  is  not  decided  on
merits,  and  therefore,  fresh  arbitration
proceedings can be undertaken. However, since
the High Court has not given any such liberty,
the petitioner(s) would suffer irreparable lose.
8. In our view, as the Award has been set aside
for  want  of  reasons,  prima  facie,  the
petitioner(s)  are  at  liberty  to  invoke  the
provisions of Section 11(6) of the 1996 Act for
appointment of an arbitrator. In such view of
the  matter,  we  decline  to  entertain  these
special leave petition(s).
9. However, since we notice that by order dated
28.04.2017, this Court, while issuing notice on
the special leave petition(s), had stayed the
operation of the impugned order, we deem it
3

--- Page 458 ---
appropriate to observe that if the petitioner(s)
apply under sub-Section (6) of Section 11 of the
1996 Act for appointment of an arbitrator, the
said  application  shall  not  be  dismissed  on
ground of limitation provided it is submitted
within a period of two months from today.
10. With  the  aforesaid  observations/liberty,
these Special Leave Petition(s) are disposed of.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall also
stand disposed of. 
               
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 459 ---

ABHINAV GUPTA                                      Respondent(s)
IA No. 135053/2025 - EX-PARTE STAY
 
Date : 15-07-2026 These matters were called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Dinesh Kumar Gupta, AoR
  

--- Page 460 ---
                   Ms. Supriya Juneja, AoR
                   Ms. Arya Suresh Nair, Adv.
  
  
                   Ms. Diksha Rai, AoR
                   Ms. Atiga Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Purvat Wali, Adv.
                   Ms. Sagun Srivastav, Adv.
  
  
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Prashant Kumar, AoR
                   Mr. Devendra Kumar Shukla, AoR
  
                            O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. Application seeking extension of time, is taken on record.
3. The respondent has filed an application seeking extension of  
time to pay the remaining installments, which was agreed to between  
the parties during the settlement and not only duly signed but also  
brought on record.
4. As per the request, the time schedule which was initially agreed 
to, is quoted below for ready reference.
“ That it is most respectfully submitted that in  
terms of the “Settlement Terms”, the respondent  
was required to pay a sum of Rs. 1,50,00,000/-  
(Rupees One Crore Fifty Lakhs Only) in seven  
installment as per following schedule:
1. On 18 th December, 2025: 1 st installment of Rs.  
5,00,000/-
2. On 20 th January, 2026: 2 nd  installment of Rs.  
10,00,000/-
3. On 20 th February, 2026: 3 rd installment of Rs.  
10,00,000/-
4. On 20 th March, 2026: 4 th installment of Rs.  

--- Page 461 ---
10,00,000/-
5. On 20 th June, 2026: 5 th installment of Rs.  
15,00,000/-
6. On 20 th July, 2026: 6 th installment of Rs.  
50,00,000/-
7. On 20 th August, 2026: 7 th installment of Rs.  
50,00,000/-”
5. However, due to various reasons, the learned counsel for the  
respondent  seeks  extension  of  two  months  for  the  remaining  
installments, which we are persuaded to allow. Accordingly, the fifth 
installment shall be paid latest by 20 th of August, 2026 of  Rs. 
15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs). The sixth installment shall be of 
Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs) shall be paid by 20th September, 
2026 and the final installment of Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs) 
shall be paid by 20th of October, 2026.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner does not object to the same. 
However, it was submitted that the Court may ensure that no further  
extension is sought by the respondent.
7. On a query to the learned counsel for the respondent, he sought 
time to take instructions.
8. Upon  the  second  call,  learned  counsel  informs  that  the  
respondent No.2 has also joined the proceedings virtually.
9. Having interacted with him, we record that he has not only  
assured the Court that he will not default but has also given an  
undertaking to pay the installments in the manner indicated above.
10. Having recorded that, the Court finds that the settlement  
arrived at between the parties needs to be approved.

--- Page 462 ---
11. In view of the aforesaid, the matter be listed 04.11.2026 at the 
top of the board.
12. Till the next date of listing, we direct that all further  
proceedings in the litigations pending inter se between the parties 
shall remain stayed.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  11493/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  15-06-2026
in BA No. 2188/2026 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi]
SULEKHA                                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE GOVT. OF NCT OF DELHI                    Respondent(s)
IA No. 181347/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 181348/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ajay Marwah, AoR
                   Mr. Swaroop Anand Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Mrigank Bhardwaj, Adv.
                   Ms. Dhriti Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Rahul Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Shavina Singh, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. D.P. Singh, ASG 
    Mr. Anmol Chandan, Adv.
    Mr. Prernal Dhal, Adv.
                   Ms. Disha, Adv.
                   Mr. S.N. Terdal, AoR
                            O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. The present petition has been filed against the impugned order
dated 15.06.2026 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi in
BA No. 2188/2026, by which the prayer for extension of the period
of interim bail granted to the petitioner on the pretext of her son
needing to undergo a surgery has been refused.

--- Page 464 ---
3. From the material on record and also confirmed by the learned
counsel for the State, the younger son of the petitioner, who is in
his mid twenties, is under a vegetative state and the Doctors
repeatedly have extended the time for the operation, which was
supposed to be performed on him, due to medical reasons.
4. Being conscious of such fact, on purely humanitarian grounds,
we consider that the prayer for extension of interim bail is
genuine.
5. Accordingly, for the present, the interim bail stands extended
till the end of October, 2026.
6. In view of the above , the Special Leave petition stands
disposed of.
7. In the meantime, it will be open to the petitioner to move an
application seeking regular bail before the Court concerned, which
shall be considered on its own merits without being prejudiced by
these orders.
8. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
    COURT MASTER (SH)                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 465 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Transfer Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  1283/2026
  NEHA CHOURASIA                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
ANAND KUMAR                                        Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed under Section 25 of the  
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”) by the petitioner  
seeking transfer of petition in Original Suit Case No. 898 of 2025,  
titled as “Anand Kumar Vs. Neha Chourasia” pending before the Court  
of Principal Family Court, Dhanbad to the Court of Principal Judge, 
Family Court, Gautam Buddha Nagar, U.P.
2. Though served, none appears for the respondent. 
3. Having gone through the material available on record and also  
the facts of the case, we are of the view that this Transfer  
Petition deserves to be allowed. Accordingly,  Original Suit Case  
No. 898 of 2025, titled as “Anand Kumar Vs. Neha Chourasia” pending  
before the Court of Principal Family Court, Dhanbad is transferred  
to the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court, Gautam Buddha Nagar,  
U.P.
4. Records shall be sent by the Court where proceedings are  
pending to the transferee Court promptly.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned courts for  
compliance.
6. The Transfer Petition stands allowed in the aforesaid terms.
1

--- Page 468 ---
7. On an application made by the parties, the Court to which the  
case is transferred shall permit the parties to appear through  
video conference on all dates except on the dates on which their  
personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
….…………………………………………………………………J
[VIPUL M. PANCHOLI]
New Delhi 
July 17, 2026

--- Page 480 ---

behalf of the respondent. Hence, the formal service of
notice on the respondent is waived.

to learned counsel for the respondent forthwith.

Rejoinder affidavit thereto, if any, be filed before the
next date of listing.

granted for this purpose.

of 2026 & 448 of 2026, registered at Civil Line Police
Station, Bilaspur, Chhattisgarh, the petitioner shall be
released on bail by the Investigating/Arresting Officer on
such terms and conditions as imposed and found to be just,
fair and reasonable.
9. However, in the attending facts, one of the conditions
shall necessarily be that if the petitioner possesses a
Passport, the same shall be deposited before the Competent
Authority/Court, until the conclusion of the Trial or
unless directed otherwise.

Investigating Officer on 27.07.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on
all such dates as he may be required.
2

--- Page 481 ---
11. Needless to add, till the investigation is not complete
in all respects, the petitioner would fully cooperate, and
if the challan/charge sheet is filed, he would maintain
good conduct and not attempt to influence any of the
witnesses in any manner till the completion of the trial.

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  3284/2025
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  24-10-2024 in
CRP(MD) No. 1916/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Madras at Madurai]
R. RAJASEKAR & ORS.                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
A.SEENIVASAN                                       Respondent(s)
[MEDIATION REPORT RECEIVED] 
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. P. Soma Sundaram, AOR
Mr. M.P.Parthiban, Adv.
Mr. Y. Arunagiri, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Siddharth Naidu, Adv. (through VC)
                   Mr. V Balachandran, Adv.
M/S.  Ksn & Co., AOR                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. There is an error in the order passed by this Court on
30.04.2026 as paragraph 1 of the said order reflects that
instead  of  Rs.5,00,000/-,  an  amount  of  Rs.50,000/-  is
mentioned in the figures.
2. The paragraph 1 of the said order is hereby corrected and
be read as under:
“1) The petitioner is further directed to deposit
Rs.5,00,000/-(Rupees Five Lakhs) in the Registry of
this Court within eight weeks, whereafter the parties
may exercise the option of mediation.”

--- Page 483 ---
2
3. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that the
demand draft of Rs.5,00,000/- is ready and will be deposited
on or before 22.07.2026 in the Registry of this Court.
4. Let the said amount be deposited in the Registry of this
Court by 22.07.2026 and the same shall be disbursed in favour
of Geetha Srinivasan, who is the daughter of respondent and on
whose behalf the respondent had initiated the proceedings
because at that point she (daughter) was not being in a
position to initiate the proceedings herself. 
5. Further, we put to the parties that whether the entire
dispute could be settled for a total sum of Rs.20,00,000/-.
After some deliberations, parties have reported to us that
they are willing to settle the dispute at Rs.20,00,000/-.
6. Out of the amount deposited earlier of Rs.10,00,000/-, a
sum of Rs.2,50,000/- along with accrued interest has already
been disbursed in favour of the respondent(s) vide order dated
22.09.2026. Remaining amount along with accrued interest if
any, will also be disbursed in favour of Geetha Srinivasan,
who is the daughter of respondent.
7. Further amount of Rs.5,00,000/- shall be paid by the
petitioner(s) directly to the account of Geetha Srinivasan,
who is the daughter of respondent, within a period of eight
weeks from today i.e. on or before 21 st September, 2026. The
account  details  of  the  wife  of  the  petitioner  shall  be
furnished by Mr. Siddharth Naidu, learned counsel for the
respondent to the learned counsel for the petitioner.

--- Page 484 ---
3
8. On payment of Rs.20,00,000/-, the matter will be treated
as settled. The impugned order of the High Court had directed
Rs.25,00,000/- to be paid in all.
9. With  a  settlement  being  arrived  at  before  us  for
Rs.20,00,000/-, the payment of Rs.20,00,000/- will be in full
and final satisfaction of the amount due and payable to the
respondent(s).
10. On the payment of Rs.20,00,000/-, the D.V.C. No.208/2014
(originally numbered as Cr.M.P.No.4499/2011) on the file of
the Judicial Magistrate (Additional Mahila Court) Madurai,
Tamil Nadu and the D.V.C. petition(s) will remain closed. 
11. An  undertaking  by  the  petitioner(s)  to  deposit  the
further amount of Rs.5,00,000/- over and above Rs.15,00,000/-
is placed on record. Any violation will result in serious
consequences.
12. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petition
is disposed of.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of. 
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

dated  11.02.2026  in  the  present  petition,  which

perused the review petition as well as the grounds in
support thereof.  We are convinced that the order
dated 20.06.2025, of which review has been sought,

O R D E R
1. An  application  (IA  No.201433/2025)  for
modification has been filed by the applicant-
appellant praying for modification of the order
dated 08.01.2025 passed in the present appeal
arising from Special Leave Petition (Crl) No.11997
of 2024, wherein it was inadvertently mentioned
that the sentence of the appellant be suspended
during the pendency of the appeal ‘ before this
Court’ instead of ‘before the High Court’. 
2. In view of the above, we modify the order
dated 08.01.2025 to the extent that the sentence
of the appellant shall remain suspended during the
pendency of the appeal before the High Court.

CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE

For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Huzefa Aziz Ahmadi, Sr. Adv.

1

--- Page 504 ---
                   Mr.  Sameer Choudhary, AOR 
                   Mr. Jaideep Gupta, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Sayid Marzook Bafaki, AOR
                   Mr. Rishi Agarwal, Av.
                   Ms. Ridhi Bose, Adv.
                   Ms. Racheeta Chawla, Adv.
                   Ms. Sampriti Baksi, Adv.                  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. These special leave petitions are directed against the interim
order dated 15.07.2026 passed by a Division Bench of the High Court
of Kerala in relation to the functioning of the State Wakf Board.
2. We have heard learned Senior Counsel for both sides.
3. In view of the opening line of paragraph 6, whereunder the
Board has been directed not to take any policy decision or incur
any capital expenditure without express leave of the Court, we are
satisfied that there is no necessity for retention of the last line
of paragraph 6 of the impugned order.
4. Consequently, the further direction issued by the High Court
to the effect that “the Board shall, for the time being, function
under  the  Supervision  of  the  Joint  Secretary  to  the  State
Government dealing with Waqf matters” is hereby deleted.
5. The Joint Secretary/Additional Secretary, being a Member of
the Waqf Board, shall continue to function in that capacity.
6. As may be seen from paragraph 8 of the impugned order, the
matter is coming up for hearing before the High Court tomorrow,
i.e., 22.07.2026.
7. We request the High Court to decide the matter expeditiously
after giving a fair opportunity to file their claim/counter claim
and hearing to the parties.
2

--- Page 505 ---
8. It is clarified that we have not expressed any opinion on the
merits of the case.
9. The Special Leave Petitions are, accordingly, disposed of.
10. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
 
(ARJUN BISHT)                               (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR

DIARY NO.38154/2026
ITEM NO.36               COURT NO.5               SECTION II-C
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) DIARY NO.38154/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 25-08-2025
in CRMP No.1380/2019 passed by the High Court of Chhattisgarh at
Bilaspur]
CHHATTISGARH ENVIRONMENT CONSERVATION 
BOARD, BHILAI Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S. JHURADABRI (QUARTZ AND SILICA)
MINING & ANR.  Respondent(s)
IA No. 191513/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 191512/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 191514/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Abhinay, AOR
                   Ms. Deepti Sarin, Adv.
                   Ms. Deeksha Prakash, Adv.
                   Mr. Pooran Chand Roy, Adv.
                   Ms. Kirti Vyas, Adv.
                   Mr. Kartik Rajpurohit, Adv.
1

--- Page 510 ---
DIARY NO.38154/2026
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned. 
2. The Regional Manager of the petitioner filed a complaint
against 1st respondent on 06.09.2010 under Sections 15 and 16
of the Environment Protection Act, 1996, Sections 37 and 40 of
the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 and
Sections 44 and 47 of the Water (Prevention and Control of
Pollution) Act, 1974. The accused filed an application for
discharge on the ground that no sanction has been obtained,
which is mandatory under the statutes referred to in the
complaint, the provisions of which are alleged to have been
violated. The jurisdictional court referred to Exhibit P3C
dated 11.09.2002 and an order dated 08.01.2010 issued by the
Member Secretary of the petitioner to reject the application
filed for discharge. 
3. The Sessions Judge allowed the revision, finding that there is
no specific sanction for the prosecution initiated; which is
mandatory. The general order relied on by the complainant was
found to be insufficient. The High Court specifically referred
to the order dated 11.09.2002 by which the Regional Officers
2

--- Page 511 ---
DIARY NO.38154/2026
were empowered to file suit on behalf of the Board; which was
incompetent insofar as a criminal proceeding is concerned. 
4. The learned Government Advocate points out Annexure P15 to
contend that there was a sanction specifically with respect to
the respondents-accused herein. A perusal of the order dated
08.01.2010 indicates that a specific reference is made to
quartzite and silica sand mine by the 1 st respondent having
been continued without obtaining an establishment consent.
There was a direction to ensure valid legal action against the
industry and inform the Board’s headquarters. This cannot be
taken as a sanction to prosecute, as required under the
statute which has to be specified, authorizing an officer to
proceed under the specific provision. 
5. We find absolutely no reason to entertain the Special Leave
Petition, however, we leave open the remedy of the State to
proceed in accordance with law after obtaining a proper
sanction. All contentions of either parties are left open. 
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of accordingly.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.      
   (CHANDRESH)                                   (POOJA SHARMA)
 ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 512 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
INHERENT JURISDICTION
REVIEW PETITION (C) NO. 9914 OF 2026
IN
 
CIVIL APPEAL   NO. 3631   OF 2026
STATE OF ODISHA & ORS.                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
GMR KAMALANGA ENERGY LIMITED                  Respondent(s)
WITH 
REVIEW PETITION (C) NO. 9915 OF 2026
IN
CIVIL APPEAL   NO. 3632 OF 2026
O R D E R
1. Applications for oral hearing are rejected.
2.  These Review Petitions are filed contending that the order
has been passed without the representation of the State of
Odisha. 
3.  Before passing the order, the Court had perused the Office
Report which clearly indicated that notices were served on the
State on various dates, but there is no appearance. In view of
the Office Report dated 19.03.2026 the Court passed the order
on 20.03.2026. For this reason, there is no error in passing
the order against which the Review Petitions are filed.
4.  In any event of the matter our order merely permitted the
appellant to amend the Writ Petition(s). We have not expressed
1

--- Page 522 ---
any opinion on the merits of the matter(s), which position is
already expressed in our order. 
5. We see no reason to entertain the Review Petitions, the
same are dismissed.
...........................J.
  (PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA)
...........................J.
  (ALOK ARADHE)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15, 2026.

Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Appellant(s) :

                   Ms. Lenina,Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA No. 144841/2026 - GRANT OF BAIL
1. Heard Mr. Abhilash M.R., learned counsel appearing
for the applicant/appellant and Ms. Anna Oommen, learned
counsel appearing on behalf of the standing counsel for
the respondent-State.
Criminal Appeal Nos.2432-2433/2026 Page 1 of 3

--- Page 537 ---
2. The applicant/appellant has been sentenced to a
fixed term of 5 years for offences under Section 304-
Part-II of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short, “the
IPC”). Originally, the conviction was under Sections 279,
337, 338 and 304-A of the IPC.  While the State preferred
an appeal before the High Court seeking punishment for
the offence under Section 304-Part II of the IPC for
which the charge had already been framed, but the Trial
Court  had  acquitted  the  appellant.   The  appellant
preferred the appeal before the Sessions Judge.  The
Sessions Judge confirmed the conviction as imposed by the
Trial Court.  The High Court heard both the appeals
together and convicted the appellant for the offence
under Section 304-Part II of the IPC and sentenced to
undergo imprisonment of 5 years.
3. The allegation is that the appellant, a bus driver,
had driven  the bus  in a  rash and  negligent manner
resulting the death of 44 individuals. Those are the
matters to be established at the time of hearing of the
appeals.
4. Considering that it is a fixed term sentence; the
appellant has undergone 10 months and keeping in mind the
law  laid  down  by  this  Court  on  parameters  for
consideration of bail for fixed sentence cases, we are
inclined  to  suspend  the  sentence  of  the
Criminal Appeal Nos.2432-2433/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 538 ---
applicant/appellant and enlarge the applicant/appellant
on bail.
5. We direct that pending appeals, the sentence of the
applicant/appellant  is  suspended  and  the
applicant/appellant is directed to be enlarged on bail
subject to the satisfaction of the Trial Court.
6. The application is accordingly disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
Criminal Appeal Nos.2432-2433/2026 Page 3 of 3

--- Page 539 ---

seeking transfer of Matrimonial Case No.59 of 2025 titled as
"Jawed vs. Baby Ujala ”, pending before the Court of Principal
Judge,  Family  Court,  Kishanganj,  Bihar  to  the  Court  of

before this Court either in-person or through an advocate.

petitioner, we are inclined to transfer the case sought for.

Matrimonial Case No.59 of 2025 titled as  "Jawed vs. Baby
Ujala”, pending before the Court of Principal Judge, Family
Court, Kishanganj, Bihar is hereby transferred to the Court of

6. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.2812/2012
M/S CGGC SOMA JV  APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
COMMERCIAL TAX OFFICER & ORS. RESPONDENTS
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.6628-6631/2012
CIVIL APPEAL NO.6632/2012
O R D E R
1. Having perused the impugned judgment dated 27.06.2011, we
notice  that  following  earlier  judgment  rendered  in  W.P.
No.12455/2005  (batch  matters),  the  judgment  impugned  in  the
present appeal, i.e., W.P. No.18479/2007 came to be disposed of.
The said judgment rendered in W.P. No.12455/2005 and connected
matters including W.P. No.16888/2011 came to be adjudicated by
this Court in C.A. Nos.8267-8271/2012 and connected matters. This
Court vide judgment dated 07.02.2018, following the Nine Judges’
Bench Judgment of this Court remitted the matters to the High
Court by observing thus:
“Having perused the impugned judgment dated 22.06.2011,
we are of the view that after the nine-Judge Bench
judgment of this Court, wherein the compensatory nature of
Entry Tax was expressly overruled, it would be difficult
for us to hear these appeals without fresh material being
placed. A co-ordinate Bench of this Court, by an Order
dated  29.03.2017,  has  in  similar  cases,  stated  that
appropriate course of action in such cases would be to
permit the appellants to file fresh petitions raising
other  issues  that  arise,  with  the  necessary  factual
background or any other constitutional/statutory issue
which arises for consideration. We may point out that the
1

--- Page 555 ---
impugned judgment itself has remanded the matters to
determine  the  nature  of  the  particular  earth  moving
vehicles and whether they could be said to be “motor
vehicle”  under  the  Motor  Vehicles  Act.  We  are  also
informed that I.As have been filed in this Court raising
other pleas. This Court has extended the interim order,
which was already obtained, and we permit the persons who
have filed those I.As to go by way of writ petitions to
the High Court within eight weeks from today. Interim
order to continue till then.
The civil appeals are, accordingly, disposed of.” 
2. A  perusal  of  the  aforesaid  order  would  indicate  that
appropriate course of action would be to permit the appellant(s)
to file fresh petition raising other issues that arise, with
necessary  factual  background  or  any  other  constitutional  /
statutory  issue which arises for consideration reiterating the
order of remand made by the High Court. Hence, we dispose of these
appeals as ordered in Civil Appeal Nos.8267-8271/2012 vide order
dated 07.02.2018. This Court has extended the interim order which
was already obtained and the said interim orders to continue for a
period of eight weeks from today.
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
.................J.
  (ARAVIND KUMAR)
.................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.
2

--- Page 556 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel appearing for respondent-husband would submit
that in view of the passport of the respondent having been
canceled, he is not in a position to travel to India, as ordered.
At this juncture, Ms. Aishwarya Bhati, learned ASG would submit
that necessary permission for one time travel from United State of
America (USA) to India, would be provided by the jurisdictional
Embassy. Her submission is placed on record.
2. Respondent-husband is at liberty to apply forthwith for an
‘Emergency Certificate’ to the jurisdictional Embassy for traveling
to India and in the event of such a request being made, same shall
be considered and appropriate one time ‘Emergency Certificate’ be
issued by the jurisdictional Embassy to enable the respondent-
husband to travel from USA to India.
3. We hereby clarify that our order dated 29.05.2026 is to be read
as “Respondent having been directed to be present” and words
appearing in paragraph No.1 “...has surreptitiously removed the
child from the jurisdiction of this country” is ordered to be
deleted.
4. List after two weeks.

BALDEV SINGH PANWAR              RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. The Transfer Petition is filed by the wife
for transferring the case bearing H.M.A. Petition
No. 1071/2022, titled “Shri Baldev Singh Panwar Vs.
Smt Sumitra ” from the Court of Principal Judge,
Family Court (East District), Karkardooma Courts,
Delhi to the Family Court, Dehradun, Uttarakhand.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. We  have  perused  the  averments  in  the
petition  and  the  objections  filed  by  the
husband/respondent. In the circumstances of the
case, the wife is entitled to a prayer as made in
the Transfer Petition.
4. Accordingly,  the  Transfer  Petition  is
allowed.
5. The parties are allowed to appear virtually
before the Trial Court on all non-essential dates
of hearing.

2
6. The Family Court is requested to expedite
the hearing and complete the matter, preferably
within six months from the date of receipt of this
order.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Permission to file Special Leave Petition(s)/appeal(s) is
granted in Diary No(s). 37705/2026.
2. Heard Mr. P.S.Patwalia, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner(s)/appellant(s)  in  SLP(Crl.)  No(s).  11361/2026
which is filed at the behest of the convicted accused. The
High Court had convicted the petitioner(s)/appellant(s) for
the offence punishable under Section 304 Part II read with
Section 149 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short, ‘IPC’)
and sentence to five years rigorous imprisonment. While doing
so, the High Court had modified the conviction as imposed by
the Trial Court from 304 Part I read with Section 149 of the
IPC and maintained conviction under Section 304 Part II read
with Section 149 of the IPC. The Trial Court had imposed a
sentence of life which has also been modified for five years
rigorous imprisonment by the High Court in appeal.
3. We have also heard Mr. Rishabh Sancheti, learned counsel
for  the  petitioner(s)/appellant(s)  in  SLP(Crl.)Diary
No.37705/2026, which is filed at the behest of the de-facto
complainant. The prayer in this petition is that the Trial
Court  judgment  ought  to  be  restored  on  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case.
4. Considering the nature of the matters, we are inclined to

--- Page 580 ---
3
grant leave in these matters.
5. Leave granted.
6. Ms. Nidhi Jaswal, learned counsel appeared for the State
in appeal(s) arising from SLP(Crl.) No(s). 11361/2026 and
Mr.S.Udaya Kumar Sagar, learned counsel appeared for the State
in appeal(s) arising from SLP(Crl.)Diary No.37705/2026.  
7. Let the Original records be called for. Upon receipt, the
digitized copy of the records be made available to both the
learned counsel for the appellants and the State in these
matters.
8. List on 27th July, 2026 for consideration of the prayer(s)
for bail in appeal(s) arising from SLP(Crl.)No.11361/2026.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)

1. The Transfer Petition is filed by the wife
for transferring the case bearing Criminal Appeal
No. 107/2019, titled “ Shubhi Jain Bindal Versus
Shobhit Bindal ” from the Court of ACJM, Meerut,
Uttar Pradesh to the Court of MM, Tis Hazari
(West), Delhi.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. We  have  perused  the  averments  in  the
petition  and  the  objections  filed  by  the
husband/respondent. In the circumstances of the
case, the wife is entitled to the prayer as made in
the Transfer Petition.
4. Accordingly,  the  Transfer  Petition  is
allowed.

perused the averments made in the transfer petition, we allow the
transfer petition in the interest of justice.
Accordingly, Hindu Marriage Petition No. 1877/2024, titled
“Suryabhan Singh vs. Divya Singh” , pending before the Court of the
Family Court, Ahmedabad, Gujarat , is directed to be transferred to
the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Shahjahanpur, Uttar
Pradesh. 
Records of the case shall be immediately transferred to the
transferee Court.

transferee Court on 03.09.2026. On the said date and thereafter,
the parties may appear in person or through their authorized
representatives or through video-conferencing, if the said facility
is available in the transferee Court and is permitted by it. In
case there is non-appearance on behalf of any party on the date
1

--- Page 588 ---
fixed by this Court, notice shall be issued by the transferee Court
to the defaulting party.
The parties may explore the possibility of settlement through
mediation. The transferee Court may obtain the desire and wishes of
the parties in this regard and proceed accordingly.
The transferee Court may decide the matter expeditiously.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

  CURATIVE PETITION (C) …………...D.No.11068 OF 2025

Y RAMANA RAO          PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
M/S TIMKEN INDIA LTD. & ORS.         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The application for permission to appear and argue in
person is rejected.
2. These Curative Petitions are defective for the reasons
stated in the Office Report. The defect was notified to the
petitioner-in-person via e-mail dated 30.05.2025 but the same has
not been rectified so far.
3. However, instead of rejecting these petitions for non-
prosecution, we have gone through the same on merits.
4. In our opinion, no case to entertain these Curative
Petitions is made out within the parameters indicated by this Court
in the case of Rupa Ashok Hurra  vs. Ashok Hurra & Anr. , (2002) 4
SCC 388.

--- Page 617 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL  NO(S).  8993/2012
PUBLIC TRUST MANDIR SHRI RAM JANKI              Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
  THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH & ANR.              Respondent(s)
ORDER
Heard the learned Senior counsel appearing for
the appellant and the learned counsel appearing for
the respondent(s).
Upon perusing the impugned judgment, we have no
difficulty  in  holding  that  the  authority  who  had
granted the permission to the appellant itself did not
have the jurisdiction to do so. The law is quite
settled that when an order which itself is a nullity
is set aside, then the question of jurisdiction of the
authority who passed the subsequent order would not
arise.
Be that as it may, the appellant before us is a
public trust. Notwithstanding this order passed by us,
liberty is given to the appellant to file a fresh
application seeking the required permission in the
manner known to law. As and when the said application
is filed, the competent Authority shall consider and
pass appropriate orders in accordance with law.
The appeal is disposed of, accordingly.   
1

--- Page 618 ---
Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  also
stand disposed of.
………………………………………..J.
                                                [M.M. SUNDRESH]
………………………………………..J.
                                               [PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
15.07.2026

                       VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent – State. 
3. The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.68/2025 dated 12.05.2025
registered  at  Police  Station  Division  No.1,
District – Pathankot, Punjab for the offences
punishable under Sections 115(2), 117(2) and 298
of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
4. Admittedly,  in  compliance  with  our  previous
order,  the  appellant  has  joined  the
investigation on 10.07.2026. 
1

--- Page 633 ---
5. Considering the aforesaid circumstances, we are
inclined to set aside the impugned order passed
by the High Court and grant anticipatory bail to
the appellant.
6. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside, and the appellant is granted anticipatory
bail, subject to the terms and conditions that
may be imposed by the concerned Trial Court. 
7. The appeal is allowed, accordingly.
8. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

a Senior Advocate is rejected as under Order XLVIII, Rule 2(2) of

relevant documents.

Petition is made out within the parameters indicated by this Court
in the case of Rupa Ashok Hurra  vs. Ashok Hurra & Anr. , (2002) 4
SCC 388.

SCC 388.

O R D E R
1. This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“Transfer the proceedings bearing HMA No. 771/2023,
titled  as  “Aswathy  Surendran  vs.  Sharath
Muralidharan”, pending before the Family Court,
Gurgaon, Haryana to the Ld. Principal Judge, Family
Court, Bengaluru”.
2.  As per the office report, respondent has been served. But
none has entered appearance on his behalf. Hence, the respondent is
proceeded ex-parte. 
3. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and HMA No.
771/2023, titled as “Aswathy Surendran vs. Sharath Muralidharan”,
pending  before  the  Family  Court,  Gurgaon,  Haryana  is  hereby
transferred to the Ld. Principal Judge, Family Court, Bengaluru.
4. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor court
for taking steps in accordance with this order.
5. On an application being made by the respondent, the Court
1

--- Page 681 ---
to which the case is transferred shall permit the respondent to
appear through video-conference on all dates except on the dates on
which his personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
6. The transfer petition stands allowed in the aforesaid
terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.22               COURT NO.10               SECTION XVII-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
CIVIL APPEAL Diary No(s). 36539/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  09-01-2026
in CA No. 21338/2015 passed by the Customs,Excise and Service Tax
Appellate Tribunal Regional Bench at Hyderabad]
M/S MAITHAN ALLOY LIMITED                          Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRINCIPAL COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS                  Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 196785/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING APPEAL
IA No. 196786/2026 - STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) Ms. Charanya Lakshmikumaran, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The submission of the learned counsel for the
appellant  is  that  the  issue  which  arises  for
consideration by this Court is “whether washing of
ore  to  remove  its  impurities  would  amount  to
treating it as to make it a concentrate?”
3. It is submitted that the aforesaid question is
pending consideration of this Court in Civil Appeal
(Diary) No(s). 25066/2026 wherein following interim
1

--- Page 684 ---
order was passed:
“1. Delay condoned.
2.  The  issue  that  arises  for  our
consideration  in  these  appeals  is:
“whether washing of ore to remove its
impurities would amount to treating it
as to make it a concentrate?”
3.  The  submission  of  the  learned
counsel for the appellant(s) is that on
identical issue earlier the Tribunal
had treated the imported goods as an
ore and not a concentrate; whereas, by
the impugned order, the same has been
treated as a concentrate by placing
reliance on explanatory notes of HSN
(Harmonized System of Nomenclature). It
is  submitted  that  even  in  the
explanatory note in Chapter 26 of HSN,
it is stated that for the purposes of
headings  26.01  to  26.17,  the  term
‘concentrate’  applies  to  ores  which
have had part or all of the foreign
matter removed by special treatments,
either  because  such  foreign  matter
might hamper subsequent metallurgical
operations or with a view to economical
transport.  It  is  the  case  of  the
appellant(s) that there is no evidence
in respect of those special treatments
and, therefore, the import ought to
have been accepted as an ore.
4. Issue notice, returnable in eight
weeks.
5. In the meantime, the interim order,
2

--- Page 685 ---
if any, which the appellant(s) were
enjoying during the pendency of the
proceedings before the Tribunal, shall
continue.”
4. Issue notice.
5. Tag  with  Civil  Appeal  (Diary)  No(s).
25066/2026.
6. In the meantime, the interim order, if any,
which the appellant(s) were enjoying during the
pendency of the proceedings before the Tribunal,
shall continue.
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (DIVYA BABBAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 686 ---

1. The  petitioner  is  short  by  0.5  cm  in
prescribed height to be considered eligible for
1

--- Page 687 ---
appointment on the post of Constable (Executive)
Male in Delhi Police.
2. A new ground has been taken before us, which
is, that paragraph 2(d) of General Instructions
for Recruitment Board applicable to the employees
of the Central government, vide Office Memorandum
(O.M.) dated 20.05.2015, provides for rounding
off the height to the next higher digit if the
difference is 0.5 cm or less.  It has been
submitted that the aforesaid Instructions were
relied  by  this  Court  in  its  order  dated
13.04.2026  passed  in  Civil  Appeal  Nos  4423-
4426/2026 @ SLP (C) Nos 13390-13393/2026 ( Union
of India & Ors. vs. Atul Kumar Verma & Ors .) and
are applicable to the service concerned.
3. As the aforesaid ground was taken neither
before the High Court nor before the Tribunal, we
deem it appropriate to dispose of this Special
Leave  Petition  by  giving  liberty  to  the
petitioner to apply for review of the order
passed by the High Court.
4. If the review is presented within three
weeks from today, the same shall not be dismissed
on ground of delay. Thereafter, if the petitioner
2

--- Page 688 ---
is aggrieved by the order passed in the review,
it will be open for the petitioner to file a
fresh petition before this Court against the
review order as well as the impugned order.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

ARBITRATION PETITION NO.44/2026
BROADBAND TELECOM, INC. PETITIONER(S)
                              VERSUS
BANKAI INFOTECH LIMITED RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed under Section 11(6)(a)  
read  with  Section  11(12)(a)  of  the  Arbitration  and 
Conciliation Act, 1996 (Act), for the appointment of a  
Sole Arbitrator.
2. The  petitioner  is  a  company  that  has  been  
incorporated in California, USA, whereas the respondent  
is an Indian company based in Ahmedabad. It seems that  
both parties had initially suggested their respective  
arbitrators,  but  the  suggestions  were  mutually  
unacceptable.
3. The petitioner had thus approached this Court, due to 
the prevailing difference of opinion with respect to the  
choice of the Arbitrator.
4. Upon  notice  being  issued,  the  learned  Senior  
Counsel/counsel for the parties have jointly submitted  
that Hon’ble Mr. Justice A.K. Sikri, a Former Judge of  
this Court, be appointed as the sole Arbitrator. Ordered  
accordingly.
5. The parties are directed to contact the office of  
Hon’ble Mr. Justice A.K. Sikri for initiation of the  
formal proceedings. Since the respondent has not filed  
any counter affidavit, it is clarified that all the  
objections  raised  on  its  behalf  are  kept  open  and 
permitted to be raised before the learned Arbitrator.
6. The  learned  Arbitrator  shall  affix  his  
1

--- Page 696 ---
remuneration/fees keeping in view the Fourth Schedule of  
the Act.
7. The petition is, accordingly, disposed of.

(SURYA KANT)
............................J. 
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI)
............................J. 
(V. MOHANA)

--- Page 700 ---
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. We have heard learned senior counsel for the petitioner(s) and
learned counsel for the State of Tamil Nadu.
3. We see no ground to interfere with the impugned judgment(s) of
the High Court. The measures directed by the High Court for
protecting  the  elephant  corridors  need  to  be  given  effect.
However, the State shall be at liberty to identify the land which
is worth acquisition and is not owned or possessed by the tribal
communities.  In case the State of Tamil Nadu faces any difficulty
in the acquisition process, it shall be at liberty to approach the
High Court for suitable modification.
4. However, all those petitioners who, by virtue of the High
Court judgment, were deprived of their livelihood may approach the
State  Government  for  providing  some  alternative  source  of
livelihood. Such a request shall be considered sympathetically. We,
however, make it clear that no commercial activity whatsoever shall
be allowed to run in the subject area.
5. The Special Leave Petitions are, accordingly, disposed of with
pending application(s), if any.
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR

2. We have heard learned senior counsel/counsel appearing
for the respective parties.
3. Even though we are not inclined to interfere with the
impugned judgment/order, we direct that hearing in Civil
Suit bearing No.3447 of 2021, pending before the Court of
the  learned  Civil  Judge  (Senior  Division),  Gurugram,
Haryana, be expedited.
4. Since the Civil Suit is of the year 2021, we request the
Trial Court to positively decide the same within a period
of one (01) year from today.
5. The parties undertake to fully cooperate and not take
any unnecessary adjournment.
6. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands disposed
of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
  (D. NAVEEN)                        (ANU BHALLA)
        COURT MASTER (SH)                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
   INHERENT JURISDICTION
REVIEW PETITION (CIVIL) NO.         OF 2026
(Arising from Diary No.33494/2025)
IN 
CIVIL APPEAL NO.   18802/2017  
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ORS.                  PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
DR. BHIM RAO AMBEDKAR 
VICHAR MANCH BIHAR, PATNA     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Despite the fact that there is a delay of 309
days in filing the review petition for which no
satisfactory explanation has been furnished, we have
gone through the review petition. In our opinion, no
case for review of Judgment dated 15.07.2024 is made
out. The review petition is dismissed on the ground
of delay as well as on merits.
....................,J.

1. As per the Office Report dated 30.06.2026, the  Review
Petition was found defective and the reasons thereof are
pointed out in the said report. Despite this Registry’s letter
dated 29.05.2026 to the counsel for the petitioners to cure
the defects, the defects have not been cured so far.
